EDCS-13000439.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Florentius Pompeianus, with his age, death date, and provincial year, dedicated by his brothers and sons in the 4th century CE.

EDCS-13000441.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Favia Victoriola lived 22 years and died on the 10th day before the Kalends of January, in the 308th year of the province; her husband made [this].
EDCS-13000441.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Favia Victoriola, noting her age, death date, and provincial year, dedicated by her husband in the 4th century CE.

EDCS-13000442.text_edition: \n Pro salute atque incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Impperatorum Constanti et Constans sic Auggustorum murum et portam novam et turres a solo Statulenius Felix dispensator una cum primores sic dedicavit provinciae CCCV\n
EDCS-13000442.translation: For the health and safety of our lords Emperors Constans and Constantius the Augusti, Statulenius Felix, the steward, together with the leading men of the province, dedicated the new wall, gate, and towers from the ground up in year 305 of the province.
EDCS-13000442.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a new wall, gate, and towers by a steward and local leaders in the 305th year of the province, likely to honor the emperors Constantius and Constans.

EDCS-13000473.translation: To the divine shades, the memorial of Titia Florentina, who lived 40 years, died on the third day before the Kalends of August, in year 268 of the province.
EDCS-13000473.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Titia Florentina, recording her age at death and the provincial year 268.

EDCS-13000551.text_edition: \n Diis Prosperis Mauris Salutaribus Caius Fannius Iunianus a militiis praefectus cohortis Sardorum votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-13000551.translation: To the prosperous and health-giving gods of the Moors, Gaius Fannius Iunianus, prefect of the cohort of Sardinians, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13000551.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gaius Fannius Iunianus, prefect of the Sardinian cohort, to the gods of the Moors.

EDCS-13000577.translation: The memorial of Crepereius Rufus, who lived 70 years, died on the seventeenth day before the Kalends of May, in year CC... of the province.
EDCS-13000577.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Crepereius Rufus, recording his age at death and the incomplete provincial year CC...

EDCS-13000591.translation: Pompeia Messa lived 25 years. Taurus, a centurion, for his dearest wife.
EDCS-13000591.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Taurus, a centurion, for his wife Pompeia Messa, who died at age 25.

EDCS-13000592.text_edition: \n Quinto Sittio Maximo regi sacrorum bono et iustissimo viro amatori patriae priori principi civitatis nostrae Marcus Aurelius Victor princeps praetori agens in eodem sacrimonio dignissimo raro amico una cum universis simpliciis et popularibus\n
EDCS-13000592.translation: Marcus Aurelius Victor, princeps and praetor, serving in the same religious office, to Quintus Sittius Maximus, king of the sacred rites, a good and most just man, a former lover of our fatherland, the chief man of our city, his most worthy and rare friend, together with all the simple folk and citizens.
EDCS-13000592.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Marcus Aurelius Victor to Quintus Sittius Maximus, honoring him as a distinguished civic and religious leader.

EDCS-13003605.text_edition: \n II praefectus gentis Salassum vixit annos LXX S ossa tibi bene quiescant\n
EDCS-13003605.translation: Second prefect of the Salassian people, lived 70 years. May your bones rest well.
EDCS-13003605.summary: This inscription records the age of the second prefect of the Salassian people and includes a wish for his peaceful repose.

EDCS-13003606.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Musteolus Saturninus vixit annos LXX hic situs ossa tibi bene quiescant\n
EDCS-13003606.translation: To the divine shades, Musteolus Saturninus, lived 70 years. Buried here, may your bones rest well.
EDCS-13003606.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Musteolus Saturninus, recording his age and expressing a wish for his peaceful rest.

EDCS-13003607.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nivalis Aulatis vixit annos LXXXI\n
EDCS-13003607.translation: To the divine shades, Nivalis Aulatis, lived 81 years.
EDCS-13003607.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Nivalis Aulatis, recording his age at death.

EDCS-13600148.text_edition: \n Caelesti Augustae Caius Iulius Ingenuus votum solvit libens animo anno provinciae CCXI\n
EDCS-13600148.translation: Gaius Julius Ingenuus fulfilled his vow to Caelestis Augusta willingly, in the 211th year of the province.
EDCS-13600148.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gaius Julius Ingenuus to Caelestis Augusta in the 211th provincial year.

EDCS-13600152.text_edition: \n Fortunae Reduci pro salute domini nostri Invictissimi ac Felicissimi Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti deccuriones et municipes Regienses devoti numini maiestatique eius curante Lucio Luta\n
EDCS-13600152.translation: To Fortuna Redux, for the safety of our lord, the most invincible and most fortunate Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, the decurions and municipals of Regium, devoted to his divine majesty, erected this, with Lucius Luta in charge.
EDCS-13600152.summary: A dedication to Fortuna Redux for the emperor's safety, set up by local officials of Regium.

EDCS-13600161.text_edition: \n imo issimo et ntissimo ntius SAMO CORIB qum sic AVcator dignissimo ac simo praesidi absentia dignitat-- \n
EDCS-13600161.translation: To the most excellent and most sacred Anthony, the most worthy advocate and most excellent governor, in his absence...
EDCS-13600161.summary: A partially preserved inscription honoring a governor named Anthony, likely from a public building or monument.

EDCS-13900528.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ceterisque diis deabusque pro salute atque incolumitate victoriisque domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Cai Messi Quinti Traiani Deci Pii Felicis Augusti et Herenniae Etruscillae Augustae matris castrorum coniugis domini nostri Imperatoris Deci Augusti totiusque domus divinae eorum\n
EDCS-13900528.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and all the other gods and goddesses, for the safety, well-being, and victories of our lord, Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius Pius Felix Augustus, and Herennia Etruscilla Augusta, mother of the camp, wife of our lord Emperor Decius Augustus, and of their entire divine family.
EDCS-13900528.summary: A public prayer for the safety and victories of Emperor Decius, his wife Etruscilla, and their family, likely from a temple or shrine.

EDCS-13900621.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Marci Antonini Germanici Sarmatici filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini n<e=I>pote sic idivi sic Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VII imperatori XI consuli II patri patriae et Imperatori Caesari Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici pontificis maximi fortissimi felicissimi filio Aureli Antonini Germanici Sarmatici nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Antonini abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici divi Nervae adnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto et Lucio Septimio Getae Caesari colonia Nerviana Augusta Martialis veteranorum Sitifensium VII \n
EDCS-13900621.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Germanicus Sarmaticus, brother of the deified Commodus, nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-nephew of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 7th time, Imperator for the 11th time, Consul for the 2nd time, Father of the Fatherland; and to Emperor Caesar, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus, Pontifex Maximus, the most valiant and fortunate, Aurelius Antoninus Germanicus Sarmaticus, nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-nephew of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-nephew of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-nephew of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus; and to Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar, by the colony Nerviana Augusta Martialis of the veterans of Sitifis, the 7th legion.
EDCS-13900621.summary: A complex imperial titulary inscription, likely erected by a Roman colony, honoring emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta.

EDCS-15000063.text_edition: \n Iulia Bodine reginae Uraniae liberta hic sita est\n
EDCS-15000063.translation: Iulia Bodine, freedwoman of Queen Urania, lies here.
EDCS-15000063.summary: A funerary inscription for a freedwoman named Iulia Bodine, who served Queen Urania.

EDCS-15100049.text_edition: \n us aedilis IIvir E quinquennalis eques Romanus vixit annos LXXX Marcus Coc Ianus gener et heres eius socero piissimo dedit VII Kalendas Maias (anno) provinciae CCXV\n
EDCS-15100049.translation: Aedile, twice Duumvir, and quinquennial duumvir, an equestrian of Rome, lived 80 years. Marcus Coccius Ianus, his son-in-law and heir, dedicated this to his most pious father-in-law on the 7th day before the Kalends of May, in the 215th year of the province.
EDCS-15100049.summary: A funerary inscription for a local magistrate who served multiple terms, dedicated by his son-in-law and heir.

EDCS-15100057.text_edition: \n Divo Hadriano conditori municipii secundum decretum ordinis statuam pedum VII quam municipes municipii Aelii Chobae pecunia publica decreverant auctis pedibus \n
EDCS-15100057.translation: A statue of seven feet, decreed by the town council for the deified Hadrian, founder of the municipality, according to the order, which the municipals of Aelia Choba had commissioned from public funds, with additional feet added.
EDCS-15100057.summary: A record of the commissioning and erection of a statue honoring Emperor Hadrian, the founder of the municipality, by its citizens.

EDCS-15100119.text_edition: \n Igne create Liber dextra servate Tonantis et deus in deo caute et Semeleia mater Lucius Tettius Saturninus sacerdos vota libens solvit kara sic cum coniuge\n
EDCS-15100119.translation: Created by fire, Liber, protect the right hand of the Thunderer, and Semeleia, mother of gods. Lucius Tettius Saturninus, priest, willingly fulfilled his vows, together with his wife.
EDCS-15100119.summary: A dedication to Liber (Bacchus) and Semeleia, possibly by a priest, invoking divine protection.

EDCS-15500058.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus Brittanicus maximus securitati provincialium suorum consulens turres novas instituit et veteres refecit opera militum suorum curante I Perpetuo procuratore suo\n
EDCS-15500058.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus Britannicus Maximus, concerned for the security of his provinces, established new towers and repaired old ones with the work of his soldiers, with his procurator I. Perpetuus in charge.
EDCS-15500058.summary: An inscription recording Emperor Commodus's infrastructure project of building and repairing military towers for provincial security.

EDCS-15600024.text_edition: \n Pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi [Alexan][dri] Augusti et Iuliae [Mamaeae matris] Augusti nostri totiusque domus divinae Caius Iulius Peregrinus duplicarius alae Flaviae morans at sic fauces Vazub cum equitibus Fortunato et Muciano votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-15600024.translation: For the safety of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus [Alexander] Augustus and Julia [Mamaea, mother] of our Augustus, and of their entire divine family, Gaius Iulius Peregrinus, a duplicarius of the ala Flavia, stationed at the Fauces Vazubae, with the cavalrymen Fortunatus and Mucianus, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-15600024.summary: A votive offering for the safety of Emperor Alexander Severus and his mother Julia Mamaea, made by a soldier and fellow cavalrymen.

EDCS-15600025.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantio Invi<c=O>to Pio Felici Vagusto sic et Flavio Valerio Severo nobilissimo Caesari res publica Dianensium \n
EDCS-15600025.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, and to Flavius Valerius Severus, most noble Caesar, by the state of the Dianensians.
EDCS-15600025.summary: This is a public dedication to Emperors Constantius and Severus by the city of Dianensium, likely erected during their joint rule in the 4th century CE.

EDCS-15600026.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Marco Aurelio Num[eriano Augusto ------\n
EDCS-15600026.translation: To our lord Marcus Aurelius Numerianus Augustus [lacuna].
EDCS-15600026.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Numerian, dating to his reign in the late 3rd century CE, with the remainder of the text lost.

EDCS-15600027.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Pii Severi [nepoti] divi Magni Antonini Pii [filio] Marco Aurelio Severo Alex[an]dro Pio Felici Augusto consuli II patri patriae proconsuli a Thamallula milia passuum I\n
EDCS-15600027.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, grandson of the deified Pius Severus, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, one mile from Thamallula.
EDCS-15600027.summary: This milestone records a road measurement of one mile from Thamallula, dedicated to Emperor Severus Alexander, who reigned in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-15600028.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Claudio Tacito Pio Felici imperatori pontifici maximo O milia passuum I bono rei publicae nato\n
EDCS-15600028.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix, chief priest, one mile, born for the good of the state.
EDCS-15600028.summary: This milestone indicates one mile of road and honors Emperor Tacitus, who reigned briefly in 275-276 CE.

EDCS-15900386.text_edition: \n Domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Iuli Phil[ippi] Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti a Timici milia passuum II\n
EDCS-15900386.translation: To our lords, the Emperors Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, two miles from Timici.
EDCS-15900386.summary: This milestone records a road measurement of two miles from Timici, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab, who reigned in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-15900390.text_edition: \n Severo Pertinaci Invicto Augusto statuam quam pro honore aedilitatis Publi Valeri Longi iunioris fili sui promisit Publius Valerius Longus princeps Publi Valeri Longi principis filii posuit\n
EDCS-15900390.translation: Publius Valerius Longus, son of Publius Valerius Longus, princeps, erected this statue for Severus Pertinax Invictus Augustus, which Publius Valerius Longus the younger, son of his father, promised for the honor of his aedileship.
EDCS-15900390.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue to Emperor Pertinax by Publius Valerius Longus in honor of his son's aedileship, likely dating to the late 2nd century CE.

EDCS-16200699.text_edition: \n Lucio Decio Luci filio Quirina Honorato ex decurione veterano aedili IIviro IIviro quinquennali item aedili IIviro IIviro quinquennali Tigavitanorum Rusgunienses et Rusgunis consistentes ob merita aere collato quod annonam frumenti passus non sit increscere posita (anno) provinciae CXXV\n
EDCS-16200699.translation: To Lucius Decius Honoratus, son of Lucius, of the voting tribe Quirina, from a veteran decurion, aedile for the second time, duovir, duovir quinquennalis, also aedile, duovir, duovir quinquennalis, by the Tigavitanorum and the Rusgunenses residing in Rusgunia, from a communal fund because he did not allow the price of grain to increase, erected in the provincial year 125.
EDCS-16200699.summary: This dedication honors Lucius Decius Honoratus for his public service, including managing the grain supply, and was funded by the communities of Tigavitanorum and Rusgunenses, dated to the provincial year 125 (likely 3rd century CE).

EDCS-16200700.text_edition: \n Numinibus iuvenes ob pulsum moenibus hostem maximo caelicolum regi Iovi Summo Tonanti denotato genio et Maurae decoratae triumfo Centurius vota simul et Fannia proles Himerius pariter pariter Reburro creatus et duo concordes Iulii felicissimi semper ex sua non parva conlata pecunia fratres has saepes laeti statuunt quo sederet alma stat virtute fremens Ultrix Victoria diva quaeque suo nutu spem pacis laeta promittit stat quoque pro templis alacer Cyllenius istis consecratque locum et votum devovet aris\n
EDCS-16200700.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Thunderer, king of the celestials, and to Maura, adorned with triumph, the centurion and Fannia, offspring Himerius, and the two harmonious Julii, his happiest brothers, ever, from their own considerable contribution of money, gladly set up these fences, so that the nurturing, rageful goddess of Victory may dwell here with her strength, and she who with her nod happily promises the hope of peace; and also for the temples, he, the zealous Cyllenius, consecrates the place and dedicates a vow at the altars.
EDCS-16200700.summary: This complex dedication honors Jupiter, Maura, and Victory, set up by a centurion, Fannia, Himerius, and two brothers named Julius, likely related to military service and local cults.

EDCS-16200961.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Luci filio Helvio Secundo praefecto fabrum Romae adlecto a divo Nerva in quinque decuriis praefecto cohortis equitatae II Bracarorum Augustanorum iterum praefecto cohortis I Flaviae civium Romanorum equitatae tribuno legionis IIII Scythicae iterum tribuno legionis XII fulminatae praefecto equitum alae Phrygum iterum praefecto alae II Gallorum scribae decuriarum quaestoriae et aedilium curulium Caesariensi quem absentem cives sui omnibus magistratuum honoribus publico decreto exornaverunt Caesarienses decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-16200961.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Helvius Secundus, son of Lucius, prefect of the engineers in Rome, appointed by the deified Nerva to the five decuries, prefect of an auxiliary cavalry cohort, again prefect of an auxiliary cohort of Roman citizens, tribune of the IV Scythica legion, again tribune of the XII Fulminata legion, prefect of cavalry of the Phrygian ala, again prefect of the II Gallorum ala, scribe of the quaestorian and curule aedile decuries, a Caesariensis whom his fellow citizens honored with all magistracies by public decree, the Caesarienses by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-16200961.summary: This inscription lists the extensive military and civil career of Tiberius Claudius Helvius Secundus, who was honored by his fellow citizens of Caesarea with public decrees.

EDCS-16201032.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Thunderer, by Dio.
EDCS-16201032.summary: This is a short dedication to Jupiter by an individual named Dio.

EDCS-16201181.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Honorio sacerdotali curatori RE rei publicae bis perpetuo Aedinia Valeria una cum Iuliis Principino et Commodo liberis suis recordationis et pietatis causa hunc memoriae titulum perennem posuit et dedicavit marito carissimo vixit annis XXXXII set sic vixit decessit pridie Nonas Octobres anno provinciae CCIXXII\n
EDCS-16201181.translation: For Gaius Julius Honorius, former priest and twice perpetual curator of public affairs, Aedinia Valeria, together with her children Julius Principinus and Commodus, for the sake of remembrance and devotion, erected and dedicated this everlasting memorial tablet to her dearest husband, who lived 42 years; he lived thus, he died the day before the Nones of October in the 222nd year of the province.
EDCS-16201181.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a wife and children for a deceased husband and father, a prominent local official, dated to the 222nd provincial year.

EDCS-16201226.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae colonia Iulia Augusta Rusazu legionis VII immunes VIIII\n
EDCS-16201226.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, Invictus, Pius, Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland; the colony Iulia Augusta Rusazu [dedicated this in honor of] the nine legionary immune soldiers.
EDCS-16201226.summary: This is a dedication from the colony of Rusazu to Emperor Philippus and nine legionary immune soldiers, likely dating to Philippus' reign.

EDCS-16201555.text_edition: \n Diis patriis deabusque Fortunae Reduci pro salute atque incolumitate domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris Publi Licini Gallieni Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti Marcus Aurelius Victor vir egregius praeses provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis protector eius provinciae CCXXIIII Kalendis I\n
EDCS-16201555.translation: For the ancestral gods and goddesses, and for the Fortune Who Brings Return, for the health and safety of our lord the Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus, Invictus, Pius, Felix Augustus, Marcus Aurelius Victor, distinguished man, governor of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis and protector of that province, [dedicated this] on the 4th day before the Kalends of January [in the year] 224.
EDCS-16201555.summary: This is a dedication by Marcus Aurelius Victor, governor of Mauretania Caesariensis, to the gods and Emperor Gallienus for the emperor's safety, dated to the 224th year.

EDCS-16201696.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis III consuli patri patriae Ausu<n=II>ubtenses devoti numini eius milia XXI \n
EDCS-16201696.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, Invictus, Pius, Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul, father of the fatherland; the people of Ausunubtes, devoted to his divine will, [dedicated this] for 21 miles.
EDCS-16201696.summary: This is a dedication from the people of Ausunubtes to Emperor Gordian III for 21 miles, likely a road marker, dating to his third tribunician power year.

EDCS-16201715.text_edition: \n Infatigabili indulgentia domini nostri Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti auctis viribus et moenibus suis castellani Citofactenses muros exstruxerunt curante Licinio Hieroclete procuratore Augusti praeside provinciae anno provinciae CLXXXVIII\n
EDCS-16201715.translation: With tireless indulgence of our lord Severus Alexander, Pius, Felix Augustus, with increased forces and its walls, the castellans of Citofactenses built the walls, under the supervision of Licinius Hierocles, procurator of the Emperor and governor of the province, in the 188th year of the province.
EDCS-16201715.summary: This records the construction of walls by the castellans of Citofactenses, with supervision from the provincial governor and procurator, dated to the 188th provincial year and likely honoring Emperor Severus Alexander.

EDCS-16300567.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinace et Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Antonino [] [[]] [[]] [] Auggustis Rusucuritani III\n
EDCS-16300567.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus [Augusti], Rusucuritani [dedicated this] for 3 miles.
EDCS-16300567.summary: This is a dedication likely marking a road section of 3 miles, honoring co-emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, with the dedicants being the Rusucuritani.

EDCS-16300569.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus Germanicus maximus tribunicia potestate XV consul III per Celsum procuratorem suum restituit\n
EDCS-16300569.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, restored [this] through his procurator Celsus.
EDCS-16300569.summary: This records the restoration of something by Emperor Caracalla, who held extensive titles and was in his 15th tribunician power year and 3rd consulship, acting through his procurator.

EDCS-16300570.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus pater patriae tribunicia potestate consul proconsul a Lucu milia passuum VI\n
EDCS-16300570.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, father of the fatherland, with tribunician power, consul, proconsul, [built/restored this] for 6 miles from Lucu.
EDCS-16300570.summary: This marks a road of 6 miles from Lucu, attributed to Emperor Severus Alexander in his capacity as consul and proconsul.

EDCS-16300571.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli nepoti divorum Gordianorum a Tigit milia passuum X\n
EDCS-16300571.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, Pius, Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, nephew of the divine Gordians, [built/restored this] for 10 miles from Tigit.
EDCS-16300571.summary: This marks a road of 10 miles from Tigit, dedicated to Emperor Gordian III, highlighting his familial connection to previous Gordians, and likely built or restored under his authority.

EDCS-16400103.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus optimus maximus tribunicia potestate pater patriae consul consul VI a Tasaccura ad Regias milia passuum XXVI\n
EDCS-16400103.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the deified Nerva, the best and greatest, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul for the 6th time, [built/restored this] for 26 miles from Tasaccura to Regias.
EDCS-16400103.summary: This marks a road of 26 miles from Tasaccura to Regias, dedicated to Emperor Trajan, emphasizing his imperial titles and sixth consulship.

EDCS-16400309.text_edition: \n Res publica Med Publius Aufidius Aufidianus et Lucius Arruntius Celsus maiores iuvenum de suo fecerunt anno provinciae CCIIII\n
EDCS-16400309.translation: Publius Aufidius Aufidianus and Lucius Arruntius Celsus, leaders of the youth, made this from their own resources in the 204th year of the province.
EDCS-16400309.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a public work, likely built by local officials for the community in a Roman province.

EDCS-16500141.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII consul III proconsul Thanaradi milia passuum XXXIDCCCCX Auzia milia passuum XVIICC\n
EDCS-16500141.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, reached Thanarada, 30,490 paces, and Auzia, 1,670 paces.
EDCS-16500141.summary: This inscription marks the distances to two locations, indicating the completion of a road or a military advance under Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-16700099.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantio nobilissimo Caesari pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli res publica Tamallensium ex conlatione ERPF P posuit dedicavitq(ue)\n
EDCS-16700099.translation: The republic of Tamallum placed and dedicated this to Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius, most noble Caesar, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul.
EDCS-16700099.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the community of Tamallum to Emperor Constantius.

EDCS-16700100.text_edition: \n Iuliae Mamaeae Augustae matri domini nostri Imperatoris Caesaris divi Magni Antonini Pii filii divi Pii Severi Augusti nepotis Marci Aureli Severi Alex[andri] Pii Felicis Augusti pontificis maximi tribuniciae potestatis VI consulis II patris patriae proconsulis et castrorum decreto decurionum res publica Thamallulensium posuit\n
EDCS-16700100.translation: By decree of the castrorum and of the decurions, the republic of Thamallulenses placed this to Julia Mamaea Augusta, mother of our lord Emperor Caesar divine Antoninus Pius, son of the divine Pius Severus Augustus, grandson of Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, proconsul.
EDCS-16700100.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by the community of Thamallulenses to Julia Mamaea, mother of Emperor Severus Alexander.

EDCS-16700234.text_edition: \n Indulgentia novi saeculi Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordiani Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti restitutoris orbis kastellum Varnazanensem sic quod antehac Augusto spatio cinctum muro continebatur nunc reparatis ac fortis viribus fiducia pacis hortante ad faciem maioris loci prolatum est Faltonio Restitutiano viro egregio praeside curante Aurelio Felice viro egregio procuratore Augusti nostri\n
EDCS-16700234.translation: By the benevolence of the new age of Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, restorer of the world, the fortress of Varnazanum, which was previously enclosed by a wall in a certain space, has now been extended to the appearance of a larger place with repaired and strengthened forces, encouraged by the confidence of peace, under the supervision of Faltonius Restitutianus, an outstanding man, the president, and Aurelius Felix, an outstanding man, procurator of our Augustus.
EDCS-16700234.summary: This inscription records the renovation and expansion of a fortress under Emperor Gordian III, highlighting the administrative roles of Faltonius Restitutianus and Aurelius Felix.

EDCS-16900184.text_edition: \n Menseleum sic Salcamaris Salcamar Aulivei filius mater Gellia dulcis Ausnufae vixsit sic annis LXV fecerunt Fundanus Hedulianus fili et Vertumna anno provinciae CLXXXXII\n
EDCS-16900184.translation: Salcamaris, son of Salcamar Auliveius, and his mother Gellia, sweet Ausnufa, lived 65 years. Fundanus Hedulianus and his son Vertumna made this in the 162nd year of the province.
EDCS-16900184.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument commemorating Salcamaris and his mother, erected by his son and grandson in a Roman province.

EDCS-17100131.text_edition: \n Imperatori Victori Septimi Severi nepos sic Marci Aurelii Antonini Macni sic <f=E>(ilio) Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro Pio Felici Augusto ex auctoritate viri egregii Axi Aeliani procuratoris Augusti nostri rationis privatae coloni vici Augusti nostri aram posuerunt \n
EDCS-17100131.translation: The colonists of the vicus Augusti nostri erected this altar by the authority of the outstanding man Axius Aelianus, procurator of our Augustus, to Emperor Victor, grandson of Septimius Severus, to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.
EDCS-17100131.summary: This inscription records the erection of an altar by colonists to Emperor Severus Alexander, under the authority of the procurator Axius Aelianus.

EDCS-19400138.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the country. From Tipasitanos, 3 miles.
EDCS-19400138.summary: This inscription is a milestone indicating a distance of 3 miles from Tipasitani to Emperor Philip the Arab.

EDCS-23200005.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aemilius Proculus set this up for his mother Cornelia Didosa, who lived 58 years. In the 188th year of the province.
EDCS-23200005.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a son for his mother in a Roman province.

EDCS-23200006.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Publius Julius Agrinus lived 70 years. Lucilla his daughter made this for her deserving father with her own money. In the 168th year of the province.
EDCS-23200006.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a daughter for her father in a Roman province.

EDCS-23200007.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Flavianus, son of Maximu[s?], lived for 56 years. Flavius Ae[milius?] Modestus erected this at his own expense.
EDCS-23200007.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavianus, erected by Flavius Aemilius Modestus, likely in the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-23200018.translation: Marcus Au[...], ex-praefect, built this centenarium from his own funds and dedicated it, from its foundations, in provincial year 287.
EDCS-23200018.summary: This records the construction of a centenarium (possibly a funerary monument or a structure related to the army) by a former prefect, Marcus Au[...], in the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-23200019.translation: Hope, in the name of God, through you, Nubel, we see these things; may you possess them firmly with your good things.
EDCS-23200019.summary: This is a short, possibly religious or personal, inscription invoking hope and a person named Nubel, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-23200022.translation: To Auzio, the god Genius and Protector of the colony, Extricatus [...], Maesia [...], Institutae [...], Iucundae, his wife Honorata [...], Primosa [...], with their honors, at the urging of their father Claudius Iuvenalis and brother Decennius Victorinus, Lucius Caecilius Victorinus, a friend, dedicated this on the Kalends of January in provincial year 185.
EDCS-23200022.summary: This is a dedication to the local deity Auzio, likely by members of several families including Lucius Caecilius Victorinus, possibly a tomb inscription from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-23200023.translation: Titus Aelius Longinus, patron of the colony, having fulfilled all honors, happily dedicated this temple, honored with gifts and divine power by the most holy Celestial Augusts, according to a vow undertaken for the preservation of his house, decorated and built at his own expense, along with Aelia Longi filia Saturnina, his wife, in provincial year 171.
EDCS-23200023.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to the Celestial Augusts by the patron of the colony, Titus Aelius Longinus, and his wife, in provincial year 171 (likely 3rd century AD).

EDCS-23200024.translation: To the Holy Gods, Liber and Libera, Protectors of houses and their affairs, with their divine powers renewed, having added a tribunal with all its ornaments, Postumius Maurus with his wife Liburnia Felicia and daughters Postumia Victoria and [...] built and dedicated this with square stone at his own expense, along with Lucius Tannonius Donatus with his wife Flavia Quarta and [...], and Pomponius [...] with his wife Saturnina and son Pomponis Perpetuus and daughter Felicia and his wife Fortunata, gladly with willing hearts, on the 3rd day before the Kalends of June in provincial year 196.
EDCS-23200024.summary: This inscription details the renovation and dedication of a shrine or temple to Liber and Libera by multiple families, including Postumius Maurus and Lucius Tannonius Donatus, in provincial year 196 (likely 3rd century AD).

EDCS-23200026.translation: To the horned Panthea, joined in sacred rites with the Thunderer, who is also revered in Libyan and Moorish lands, whom Jupiter Hammon in the middle region between both sides shelters with the stern Dis, dedicates this high altar and lofty throne, and the frequent gods [...]ianus from military service, from a supplicant's vow [...] renews the mistress and twofold form.
EDCS-23200026.summary: This is a poetic dedication to a syncretic deity, Panthea, likely related to Ammon and Jupiter, possibly from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-23200027.translation: With their own resources, Felix and Calvus, giving thanks, erected this altar to the most holy god Phoebus at the time when they held the sacred fasces of the fatherland, happily.
EDCS-23200027.summary: This records the erection of an altar to Apollo by Felix and Calvus, possibly during their tenure of public office, likely in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-23200028.translation: To Pluto, Cyria, and Ceres the mother, the Holy Gods, Quintus Clodius Clodianus, patron of the colony, having completed all his public duties, established and dedicated this altar with his wife Iulia Donata and son Clodius Aprile and other daughters, fulfilling a promised vow in provincial year 281, on the 11th day before the Kalends of March.
EDCS-23200028.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to Pluto, Cyria, and Ceres by the patron of the colony, Quintus Clodius Clodianus, and his family, in provincial year 281 (late 3rd century AD).

EDCS-23200032.translation: To Victory of the Augusti. Gaius Julius Emeritus, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, quaestor, former aedile, set up this statue, which, because this honor was conferred upon him with the people's most prompt goodwill beyond the legal amount of 5,000 sesterces, [...] along with Basi [...]
EDCS-23200032.summary: This inscription records the erection of a statue by Gaius Julius Emeritus to celebrate his appointment as aedile, likely during the reign of Septimius Severus and Caracalla (early 3rd century AD).

EDCS-23200033.translation: To Victoria Augusta Sancta, goddess, Lucius Iulius, son of [---], Capito, prefect of the frontier, made and dedicated with all his people. In the province, 262.
EDCS-23200033.summary: A dedication to the goddess Victoria Augusta Sancta by Lucius Iulius Capito, a frontier prefect, dated to 262 (presumably from the province's founding).

EDCS-23200034.translation: To Virtue, the holy Augusta goddess, Publius Caelius Victor, priest, with Aurelia Germanilla, his wife, made and dedicated this tribunal, built with square masonry on their own land. In the province, 202.
EDCS-23200034.summary: A dedication to the goddess Virtue Augusta by the priest Publius Caelius Victor and his wife Aurelia Germanilla, recording the construction of a tribunal, dated to 202 (presumably from the province's founding).

EDCS-23200037.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus Maximus, descendant of the deified Nerva, Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Felix Augustus, the Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, pater patriae, with tribunician power for the sixth time, emperor for the eleventh time, consul for the second time, proconsul. The Auzienses.
EDCS-23200037.summary: A lengthy imperial titulature for Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Felix Augustus, likely part of a monument erected by the Auzienses, dating to his sixth tribunician power and eleventh acclamation as emperor.

EDCS-23200038.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo tribunicia potestate VIIII imperatori XI pontifici maximo patri patriae et [Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate IIII et Publio Septimio Getae Caesari] et Iuliae Augustae dum vivit Titus Aelius Longinus flamen perpetuus omnibus honoribus functus Publio Aelio Peregrino procuratore\n
EDCS-23200038.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the ninth time, emperor for the eleventh time, pontifex maximus, pater patriae, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the fourth time, and Caesar Publius Septimius Geta, and Julia Augusta, while she lives, Titus Aelius Longinus, perpetual priest, having completed all honors, for Publius Aelius Peregrinus, procurator.
EDCS-23200038.summary: A dedication to Emperors Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Caesar Geta, and Empress Julia Augusta, honoring Titus Aelius Longinus, a perpetual priest, and mentioning Publius Aelius Peregrinus, a procurator, likely dating to the period when Severus, Caracalla, and Geta were co-emperors.

EDCS-23200039.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Sarmaticus, [---] Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus [---], Lucius Orillius Geminus, son of Lucius, of the voting tribe Quirina, for the honor [---]
EDCS-23200039.summary: A dedication to Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, mentioning his Sarmatic title, by Lucius Orillius Geminus, likely commemorating an honor received, with lacunae obscuring full details.

EDCS-23200040.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the camp. The Auzienses.
EDCS-23200040.summary: A dedication to Julia Augusta, identified as 'mother of the camp', by the Auzienses, likely commemorating her role as empress.

EDCS-23200041.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of Caesar and of the camp.
EDCS-23200041.summary: A dedication to Julia Augusta, referred to as 'mother of Caesar and of the camp', likely referring to her status as empress and mother of the reigning emperor.

EDCS-23200042.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Felix Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, grandson of the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, descendant of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the eighth time, consul for the second time, pater patriae, proconsul.
EDCS-23200042.summary: An imperial titulature for Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), son of Septimius Severus, recording his titles and honors, including his eighth tribunician power and second consulship.

EDCS-23200043.translation: [To Publius Septimius Geta, pontifex maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, Augustus, consul], to Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Felix Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the twelfth time, emperor for the twelfth time, consul for the third time, father of his country, proconsul, and to Julia Augusta, mother of the Caesars and of the camp, [son], and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the eighth time, consul for the second time, father of his country, proconsul, [brother].
EDCS-23200043.summary: A complex dedication honoring Emperors Septimius Severus, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), and possibly Publius Septimius Geta, along with Empress Julia Augusta, with detailed imperial titles and familial relationships, dating to Severus's 12th tribunician power and 12th acclamation.

EDCS-23200046.translation: To Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Magnus Antoninus Augustus Pius, [grandson] of the deified Severus Augustus, Marcus Aurelius Severus [Alexander...]
EDCS-23200046.summary: A partial imperial titulature for Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, son of [Julia Augusta] Augusta and grandson of Septimius Severus, with lacunae obscuring the full text.

EDCS-23200047.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus.
EDCS-23200047.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Maximinus, likely an inscription on a monument or building dating to his reign.

EDCS-23200049.translation: By order of the divine majesty of Diocletian and Maximian, Augusti, a bridge, destroyed by the savagery of war, has now been restored with peace returned, through Aurelius Lituus, our most excellent governor, by the efforts of Flavius [---], his dispunctor, and of the Julii, two from Caesoratias of the province, 251.
EDCS-23200049.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a bridge destroyed by war, commissioned by Diocletian and Maximian and carried out by Aurelius Lituus, governor, and other officials, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century.

EDCS-23200050.translation: To our Lord, the Emperor Caesar Gaius Flavius Constantine, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, of the province, 273.
EDCS-23200050.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great, likely on a public building or monument, dating to his reign.

EDCS-23200053.translation: To Publius Aelius Primus, son of Publius, of the Quirina tribe, Roman knight, tribune of the 4th Cohort of Syngambri, from the first-pilo military service, tribune of the 4th Cohort of Vigiles, from decurion of the Thracian cavalry wing, commander of a detachment of Maurorum cavalry, defender of his province, decurion of three colonies, Auzium, Rusgunia, and Equizetus. Publius Aelius Primus, decurion of the colony Auzium, died before he could dedicate it. His daughter Aelia Audi made it for her most pious father and dedicated it on the 13th day before the Kalends of March, province 216.
EDCS-23200053.summary: This is a dedication for Publius Aelius Primus, a Roman knight with a distinguished military and civil career, set up by his daughter, dating to the period when he was decurion of multiple colonies.

EDCS-23200054.text_edition: \n Lucio Alfeno Senecioni procuratori Augusti ob egregiam tanti viri industriam proque singulari eius innocentia decreto decurionum omnium primo res publica municipi Auziensium\n
EDCS-23200054.translation: To Lucius Alfenus Senecio, procurator of Augustus, for his outstanding industry and singular integrity, by decree of all the decurions, the public affairs of the municipality of Auzium first...
EDCS-23200054.summary: This fragmentary inscription honors Lucius Alfenus Senecio, a procurator of Augustus, for his excellent work and integrity, with the decree of the decurions of Auzium.

EDCS-23200055.translation: To Quintus Gargilius Martialis, son of Quintus, of the Quirina tribe, Roman knight, prefect of the 1st Cohort of Astures in the province of Britannia, tribune of the Spanish Cohort in the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, from first-pilo military service, commander of the cohort of singulares and a detachment of Maurorum cavalry, decurion of the two colonies Auzium and Rusgunia, and patron of the province, for his distinguished love for the citizens and his singular affection for the fatherland, and because by his valor and vigilance the rebel Faraxen with his satellites was captured and killed. The council of the colony of Auzium, deceived by the Bavari with public money, made this. Dedicated on the 8th day before the Kalends of April, province 221.
EDCS-23200055.summary: This is a dedication to Quintus Gargilius Martialis, a Roman knight with a significant military career and a patron of the colonies of Auzium and Rusgunia, honored for his service in capturing a rebel leader, and funded by the council of Auzium.

EDCS-23200057.text_edition: \n Caium Octavium Pudentem Caesium Honoratum procuratorem Augg[gustorum] praesidem incomparabilem innocentia praecipuum omniumque virtutum virum municipem piissimum Auzienses\n
EDCS-23200057.translation: To Gaius Octavius Pudentes Caesius Honoratus, procurator of the Augusti, incomparable governor, most distinguished in integrity, a man of all virtues, most pious citizen. The Auzienses...
EDCS-23200057.summary: This is a dedication by the people of Auzium to their governor, Gaius Octavius Pudentes Caesius Honoratus, praising his virtues and integrity.

EDCS-23200070.translation: The market, with its porticoes, weights, and all its decorations, the public entity of the colony Septima Aurelia Auziensium completed and dedicated, from its own funds as well as from the contributions of the decurions and the work of the populace, starting from the foundations. Dedicated on the 18th day before the Kalends of January, province 191, under the supervision of Gaius Aufidius Victorinus and [---] Iuventius Karus, aediles, whose honorary sums were also spent on this work.
EDCS-23200070.summary: This inscription records the completion and dedication of a market with its various facilities in the colony of Auzium, funded by public money, decurions, and citizens, and built under the supervision of the aediles, dating to province 191.

EDCS-23200073.translation: Decennius Claudius Juvenalis Sardicus. Having completed the boundary markers and ovile (sheep pens), and also the tribunal of judges, which for the memory of Claudius Rufinianus, a young man of good memory, and of Rufinianus and Victorinus, his grandsons, and of Longania Primosa, a woman of good memory, his wife and mother of the Claudii, and of Kaninia Respecta, a woman of good memory, his grandmother and mother of the Claudii, province 188.
EDCS-23200073.summary: This inscription records the completion of boundary markers, sheep pens, and a tribunal of judges by Decennius Claudius Juvenalis Sardicus, dedicated to the memory of his deceased family members, dating to province 188.

EDCS-23200077.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Farewell Clodius Felix, my dearest son, who held the quaestorship of the state, and you father, may you who greet me pass safely. He lived 7 years, 2 months. Clodius Clodianus, dispunctor of the colony, and Terentia Donata made and dedicated this in the year of the province 281.
EDCS-23200077.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Clodius Felix, a young boy who died at age 7 and had held a minor state office, set up by his father and mother, dating to province 281.

EDCS-23200082.translation: Lur(ius) Aristo lived 70 years. Luria Iulia, perpetual flaminica, to her most worthy brother. [Lur(ius) Aris]to lived 20 years, by the instigation of Caius Geminius Cainus, she dedicated [this] to her parents. Year of the province 228.
EDCS-23200082.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of Lurius Aristo and his sister, Luria Iulia, who dedicated the monument to her brother and possibly parents.

EDCS-23200085.translation: Ulpianus, a man of good memory, having held all offices, is covered by this tomb after death. Beside him lies Castrensinus. To his sweetest spouse Ulpiano Siddina, who was taken from life by a pitiable fate on the fortieth day of death, he made and dedicated [this]. He lived 56 years, under the care of Sulpicianus, his son-in-law. Year of the province 207.
EDCS-23200085.summary: This funerary inscription is a tombstone for Ulpianus, who died at 56, and his son Castrensinus, and was dedicated by Ulpiano Siddina, his wife.

EDCS-23200093.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aufidius Verna the younger lived 18 years. Aufidius Barigbal, his father, made and dedicated [this] to his sweetest son. Year of the province 197.
EDCS-23200093.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aufidius Verna the younger at 18 and was dedicated by his father, Aufidius Barigbal.

EDCS-23200094.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aufidia Marisa lived 91 years. Aufidius Barigbal made and dedicated [this] to his dearest mother. Sacred to the departed spirits. Aufidius Verna lived 85 years. Aufidius Barigbal made and dedicated [this] to his sweetest father. Year of the province 197.
EDCS-23200094.summary: This funerary inscription records the deaths of Aufidia Marisa (91) and Aufidius Verna (85), both dedicated by Aufidius Barigbal.

EDCS-23200098.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Caecilia Victorina, a most pious matron of good memory, his dearest wife, Iulius Felix, her husband, made and dedicated [this]. She lived 70 years. Year of the province 208. Sacred to the departed spirits. To the memory of Iulius Felix, his father, Ian(u)arius and Lucia made and dedicated [this]. He lived 80 years. Year of the province 216.
EDCS-23200098.summary: This funerary monument commemorates Iulius Felix, dedicated by his wife, and his father, dedicated by his children.

EDCS-23200099.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Caelia Quintilla, of good memory, who lived 59 years [and some months]. Sacred to the departed spirits. To the memory of Caius Iulius Felix Acutus, most loving husband, and of Gaudentius and Servandus, dearest sons. Caelia Quintilla made and dedicated [this]. Year of the province 192.
EDCS-23200099.summary: This funerary inscription honors Caelia Quintilla, who lived 59 years, and was dedicated by her husband and sons.

EDCS-23200117.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Quintus Gargilius Campanus, my sweetest husband, Gargilia Pacata, his wife, built this mausoleum of squared work, according to the terms of his will, for which he had ordered 16,000 sesterces to be paid, adding 8,000 sesterces from her own funds, from the ground up, with all her expenses, and dedicated it within the time limit set by the will. Year of the province 194, 9th day before the Ides of August.
EDCS-23200117.summary: This funerary inscription records the construction and dedication of a mausoleum for Quintus Gargilius Campanus, funded by his wife Gargilia Pacata according to his will.

EDCS-23200119.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintus Gargilius Namefamus lived 27 years, a son of good memory. Rogata, his mother, [dedicated this]. Year of the province 207. Sacred to the departed spirits. To the memory of Caelia Rogata, who lived 73 years and 3 months. Quintus Gargilius Siddinus, her husband, made [this]. Sacred to the departed spirits. Gargilius Siddinus lived 80 years and 6 months. His sons Pacatus Donatus and Faustus Gargilia Fausta lived 7 years. [Lacuna].
EDCS-23200119.summary: This funerary monument commemorates multiple family members: Quintus Gargilius Namefamus (27), Caelia Rogata (73), Gargilius Siddinus (80), and Gargilia Fausta (7), with dedications from family members.

EDCS-23200122.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To the memory of Iulius Cicero, who lived 15 years. Sissoi made and dedicated [this] to her sweetest son, and to Sabbat(t)ra, her grandson. Sacred to the departed spirits. To the memory of Kalendonis, who lived 51 years. Iulia Sissoi made and dedicated [this] to her sweetest husband.
EDCS-23200122.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Iulius Cicero, who died at 15, and Kalendonis, who died at 51, with dedications from family members.

EDCS-23200123.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To the memory of Caius Iulius Citteus, who lived 45 years, and of Gargilia Secunda, his wife, who lived 38 years. Iulius Rogatianus made [this] for his brother-in-law. Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulius Rogatianus, of good memory, lived 50 years. Iulius Rogatianus made and dedicated [this] to his father. Year of the province 211.
EDCS-23200123.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Caius Iulius Citteus (45) and his wife Gargilia Secunda (38), and Iulius Rogatianus (50), with dedications from family members.

EDCS-23200124.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. In memory of Iulius Felicianus, who lived 61 years. When he was alive, he made and dedicated [this monument]. Year of the province 207. Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. In memory of Iulia Romanis, who lived 35 years. Iulius Felicianus made this for his wife. Iulia Silvanica made this for her sister, most beloved, and also for Caecilia Blatta, wife of Iulius Felicianus, for her most beloved husbands. She made and dedicated. Year of the province 203.
EDCS-23200124.summary: This is a double tombstone inscription from the 3rd century AD, commemorating Iulia Romanis, who died at 35, and Iulius Felicianus, who died at 61, likely a husband and wife, with the inscription detailing family relationships.

EDCS-23200129.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. For Quintianus, Extricata, Donatus, and Donatus the Younger, brothers, of good memory, most beloved sons. Acutianu s, the father, and Plautia, the mother, made this in the year of the province 225.
EDCS-23200129.summary: This tombstone, dating to 225 AD, is dedicated by parents to their deceased sons Quintianus, Extricata, Donatus, and Donatus Minor.

EDCS-23200135.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. To give back to the dead what I could since my husband's death: this tomb and sacred memorials for you, Manes. Victorinus vowed this service out of grief. Iulia Kara lies in this place; she lived 40 years, 3 months, 11 days, and leaves behind dear children and a worthy husband. He made and dedicated. Year of the province 220.
EDCS-23200135.summary: This inscription from 220 AD records the death of Iulia Kara at age 40, who is remembered by her husband Victorinus and their children, with the husband dedicating the tomb.

EDCS-23200141.translation: [In memory] of grief, he was gifted. Iulia Syra, who was also Thessalia, a most rare wife, who lived 22 years, 6 months, 7 days. Aelius Donatus, her husband, made and dedicated this. In the year of the province 195.
EDCS-23200141.summary: This tombstone, dated to 195 AD, records the death of Iulia Syra (also called Thessalia) at the age of 22 and is dedicated by her grieving husband, Aelius Donatus.

EDCS-23200170.translation: Dedicated to the spirits of the departed. In memory of Sulpicia Victoria, freedwoman of Lucius. Gaius Iulius Felix, her husband, made this tomb for his ever-loving, dearest, and most cherished wife, from whom he was separated by death. Her surviving children, numbering 5, and 10 grandchildren were deeply saddened. She died on the 7th day before the Ides of January. Year of the province 188. She lived 50 years. He fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-23200170.summary: This tombstone from 188 AD commemorates Sulpicia Victoria, the wife of Gaius Iulius Felix, who died at age 50, and notes her surviving children and grandchildren.

EDCS-23200183.translation: lived 2 years, 9 months. Of the province 204.
EDCS-23200183.summary: A tombstone fragment recording the lifespan of an individual and a provincial year, likely from a funerary monument, dated to the provincial year 204.

EDCS-23200190.text_edition: \n Balnea rura domus fecit Constantius auctor et fecit ut memorent cari de sanguine nati nam ut plene lotor discas quid sit perferre laborem est novi exempli et quae est memoranda per annos lateri iuncta viro summa data coniugi tali adque sic suum documen latum imitare priora ut nomen ut quaestus ut honor ampliet in aevum Auzias quia potens eris his per saecula vita tuque dabis civibus decus et tibi patria laudes et dabis ut superes hominum linguas inanes\n
EDCS-23200190.translation: Constantius, the founder, made the baths, the country, the houses, and he did it so that loved ones born of his blood may remember. For you, the bather, to fully learn what it is to endure hardship, it is of a new example, and what is to be remembered through the years. Joined to her husband at his side, the highest honour was given to such a wife. And thus, letting his own example follow the earlier ones, that his name, that his gain, that his honour may be increased forever. Auzias, because you will be powerful in these things through the ages, and you will give glory to the citizens and to you, fatherland, praises, and you will give them so that you may surpass empty human tongues.
EDCS-23200190.summary: An inscription from a bath complex, likely commissioned by Constantius and Auzias, praising the founder's generosity and the virtue of a wife, possibly dating to the later Roman Empire.

EDCS-23200202.text_edition: \n Diis sic deabusque consecratis universis numini Iovis Silvano Mercurio Saturno Fortunae Victoriae Caelesti diis sic Mauris Marcus Furnius Donatus eques Romanus flamen perpetuus ex praefecto gentis Masacesbenorum cum suis fecit et dedicavit\n
EDCS-23200202.translation: To all the gods and goddesses, consecrated to the divine power of Jupiter, Silvanus, Mercury, Saturn, Fortuna, Victoria, Caelestis, and the Moorish gods, Marcus Furnius Donatus, a Roman knight, perpetual flamen, formerly prefect of the Masacesbeni tribe, made and dedicated it with his family.
EDCS-23200202.summary: A dedication to various deities, including Roman and local gods, by Marcus Furnius Donatus, a Roman knight and former tribal prefect, likely from North Africa and dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23400852.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and all the gods and goddesses, for the safety, well-being, and victories of our most sacred lord, the Emperor Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and of Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of our Augustus, and of their entire divine household.
EDCS-23400852.summary: This dedication, likely from the reign of Gordian III (238-244 AD), is made to Jupiter and other gods for the safety and victories of the emperor and his family.

EDCS-23400853.translation: To the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius, father of the Emperor. Marcus Praecilius Rogatianus, son of Marcus, of the Arnensi tribe, dedicated this with his own money in honor of his principate.
EDCS-23400853.summary: This dedication honors the deified Emperor Marcus Aurelius (d. 180 AD) and was made by Marcus Praecilius Rogatianus in honor of his own magistracy, funded by himself.

EDCS-23400854.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, father of the fatherland, Titus Aelius Zabidus Tanaramusanus erected and dedicated it.
EDCS-23400854.summary: This is a dedication by Titus Aelius Zabidus Tanaramusanus to Emperor Septimius Severus, likely a public monument, dated to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-23400855.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, father of the fatherland. Gaius Julius Flam[...], veteran, formerly a centurion, dedicated it on account of the honor of the principate.
EDCS-23400855.summary: This is a dedication by Gaius Julius Flam[...], a veteran centurion, to Emperor Caracalla, son of Septimius Severus, likely a public monument, dated to Caracalla's reign (198-217 AD).

EDCS-23400856.translation: To the Pontifex Maximus, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, his brother. Gaius Julius Victor, veteran, formerly a decurion of an ala, dedicated it.
EDCS-23400856.summary: This is a dedication by Gaius Julius Victor, a veteran decurion, to Emperor Caracalla, referring to him as the brother of Geta, likely a public monument, dated to their joint reign (209-211 AD).

EDCS-23400858.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Severus Pertinax Augustus [...] proconsul.
EDCS-23400858.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription honoring Emperor Septimius Severus Pertinax, mentioning his proconsulship, likely a public monument, dated to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-23500007.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Publius Aelius Afininus, designated aedile, lived 24 years. Province year 163. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-23500007.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Aelius Afininus, who died at age 24 in the provincial year 163, likely from the Roman province of Hispania Citerior Baetica (founded 27 BC, so 163 corresponds to 136 AD).

EDCS-23500070.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Pius Pertinax Augustus, Adiabenicus.
EDCS-23500070.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus Pertinax, likely a public monument, dated to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-23500072.translation: To the invincible Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power 3, imperator 6, consul 2, proconsul, father of the fatherland, most brave. Gaius Julius Ianuarius and Lucius Cassius Augustin[us], quinquennial magistrates of the town, set this up with money collected from the people.
EDCS-23500072.summary: This is a dedication by Gaius Julius Ianuarius and Lucius Cassius Augustin[us], magistrates of Castellum, to Emperor Septimius Severus Pertinax, listing his titles and dignities, likely a public monument, dated to around 202 AD (based on tribunician power III and consul II).

EDCS-23500081.text_edition: \n Deo magnu sic Draconi sacrum Marcus Iunius Asclepiades votum solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-23500081.translation: Sacred to the great god, so to Dracon. Marcus Iunius Asclepiades fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-23500081.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Iunius Asclepiades to the god Dracon, likely dedicated at a shrine, dated to the Roman period.

EDCS-23500082.translation: To the Maurician Gods. Marcus Pomponius Vitellianus, having served in three military commands, procurator of the Emperor for tribal affairs, prefect of the German fleet.
EDCS-23500082.summary: This is a dedication to the Maurician Gods by Marcus Pomponius Vitellianus, listing his significant military and administrative career, likely from a tomb or public monument, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23500092.translation: Iulia Natalis, basket-seller, dedicated it in the provincial year 134.
EDCS-23500092.summary: This is a dedication by Iulia Natalis, a basket-seller, dated to provincial year 134 (likely 134 AD in a Roman province), possibly to a deity or as a contribution to a public building.

EDCS-23500107.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Felix Augustus, conqueror of Arabia, Adiabene, and Parthia the greatest, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. Gaius Julius Turranicus, because of the honor of his aedileship, in the 170th year of the province.
EDCS-23500107.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Septimius Severus by Gaius Julius Turranicus for the honor of his aedileship in the 170th provincial year, likely from a Roman province.

EDCS-23500109.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VI consuli II patri patriae proconsuli eqquites singulares devoti numini maiestatique eius curante Licinio Hieroclete procuratore eius praeside provinciae\n
EDCS-23500109.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The equites singulares, devoted to his divine majesty, under the supervision of Licinius Hierocles, his procurator and governor of the province.
EDCS-23500109.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the equites singulares to Emperor Alexander Severus, supervised by his procurator and governor, Licinius Hierocles.

EDCS-23500110.text_edition: \n Gneae Seiae Herenniae Sallustiae Barbiae Orbianae sanctissimae Augustae coniugi Augusti nostri eqquites singulares devoti numini maiestatique eius curante Licinio Hieroclete procuratore Augusti praeside provinciae\n
EDCS-23500110.translation: To Gnea Seia Herennia Sallustia Barbia Orbiana, most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus. The equites singulares, devoted to his divine majesty, under the supervision of Licinius Hierocles, procurator of Augustus and governor of the province.
EDCS-23500110.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Empress Orbiana, wife of the emperor, by the equites singulares, overseen by Licinius Hierocles.

EDCS-23500114.text_edition: \n Publio Aelio Publi filio Papiria Peregrino Rogato omnium virtutum viro praesidi dignissimo Marcus Popilius Palatina Nepos praefectus alae geminae Sebastenae domo Roma\n
EDCS-23500114.translation: To Publius Aelius Peregrinus Rogatus, son of Publius, of the Papirian tribe, a man of all virtues, most worthy governor. Marcus Popilius Nepos, of the Palatine tribe, prefect of the twin Sebastenian alae, from Rome.
EDCS-23500114.summary: This inscription honors Publius Aelius Peregrinus Rogatus, a governor, and is set up by Marcus Popilius Nepos, a prefect of an ala.

EDCS-23500118.text_edition: \n Tiberio Claudio Prisciano procuratori Augusti procuratori provinciae Pannoniae superioris procuratori regni Norici procuratori XX hereditatium procuratori provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis Quintus niorius Severus praefectus cohortis Sigambrorum praepositus classibus \n
EDCS-23500118.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Priscianus, procurator of Augustus, procurator of the province of Upper Pannonia, procurator of the Kingdom of Noricum, procurator of the XX hereditatium, procurator of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis. Quintus Ninnius Severus, prefect of the cohort of the Sigambri, in command of the fleets.
EDCS-23500118.summary: This inscription lists the various procuratorial and military commands held by Tiberius Claudius Priscianus, with Quintus Ninnius Severus noted as the dedicator.

EDCS-23500119.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Priscianus procurator Augusti fecit\n
EDCS-23500119.translation: Tiberius Claudius Priscianus, procurator of Augustus, made this.
EDCS-23500119.summary: This is a simple dedication by Tiberius Claudius Priscianus, identifying him as a procurator of Augustus.

EDCS-23702051.text_edition: \n Caio Octavio Pudenti Caesio Honorato procuratori Auggustorum a censibus Cornelius Primus decurio alae Thracum ex stratore eius praesidi innocentissimo\n
EDCS-23702051.translation: To Gaius Octavius Pudentius Caesius Honoratus, procurator of the Augusti in charge of the census. Cornelius Primus, decurion of the Thracian ala, his subordinate, most innocent governor.
EDCS-23702051.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Octavius Pudentius Caesius Honoratus, a procurator in charge of the census, and was set up by Cornelius Primus, a decurion.

EDCS-23702052.text_edition: \n Quinto Sallustio Macriniano procuratori Augg[gustorum] utriusque provinciae Mauretaniae praesidi suo et Quinto Sallustio Macriniano clarissimo viro filio eius commilitoni rarissimo et Quinto Sallustio Macriniano clarissimo puero nepoti eius ob insignem eorum erga se humanitatem Anullius Geta ex praefecto alae Parthorum\n
EDCS-23702052.translation: To Quintus Sallustius Macrinianus, procurator of the Augusti of both provinces of Mauretania, our governor, and to his son Quintus Sallustius Macrinianus, most distinguished man and comrade, and to his grandson Quintus Sallustius Macrinianus, most distinguished boy, because of their outstanding kindness towards him. Anullius Geta, former prefect of the Parthian ala.
EDCS-23702052.summary: This inscription honors Quintus Sallustius Macrinianus and his son and grandson for their kindness, and was set up by Anullius Geta, a former prefect.

EDCS-23702053.text_edition: \n Tra donis donato ab Imperatore Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Germanico corona aurea et corona hastis puris duabus vexillis duobus bello Germanico procuratori Augusti provinciae Moesiae superioris procuratori Augusti \n
EDCS-23702053.translation: Awarded gifts by the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Germanicus with a golden crown and a crown with two spears and two standards in the Germanic war. To the procurator of the province of Upper Moesia, procurator of Augustus.
EDCS-23702053.summary: This inscription commemorates gifts received by a procurator of Upper Moesia from Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus during a Germanic war.

EDCS-23702059.translation: To Grania Marcellina, also called Crementia. Quintus Granius Felix, former decurion of the Thracian ala, father, to his most sweet daughter. In the 223rd year of the province.
EDCS-23702059.summary: This is a dedication by Quintus Granius Felix, a former decurion, to his daughter Grania Marcellina, in the 223rd provincial year.

EDCS-24400430.translation: For the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armenicus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus, Publius Cere Saturninus, priest of the state, made and dedicated the images and the altar.
EDCS-24400430.summary: This inscription records the dedication of images and an altar by a priest for the well-being of Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Verus, likely dating to their joint reign (161-169 AD).

EDCS-24400432.translation: For the safety and well-being and victory of our most holy lord, Emperor Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus Invictus, and of Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of our Emperor, and of their entire divine household.
EDCS-24400432.summary: This inscription is a dedication for the health and prosperity of Emperor Gordian III, his empress Sabinia Tranquillina, and their imperial family, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-24400445.translation: The colonists, the people of the estate of Horrea Pardalariensia, have set up this altar and given it as a gift for the health of Emperor Caesar Publius Helvius Pertinax, holder of tribunician power, consul twice, father of the country.
EDCS-24400445.summary: This inscription records the erection of an altar by the colonists of the Horrea Pardalariensia estate, dedicated to Emperor Pertinax, likely in 193 AD.

EDCS-24400446.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus our lord, Parthicus Britannicus Maximus, consul for the fourth time, the colonists of the estate of Horrea, the Kalefacenses and Pardalarienses, consecrated an altar for his health and established the name of the settlement they founded as Aureliana Antoniniana, and dedicated it in the provincial year 174.
EDCS-24400446.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar and the naming of a settlement by colonists for the health of Emperor Caracalla, dated to provincial year 174 (likely 212/213 AD).

EDCS-24400450.translation: Eternal home for Rodius Severus, he lived 66 years in provincial year 256. Eternal home for Domitia Pacata, she lived [3] years.
EDCS-24400450.summary: This is a funerary inscription marking the eternal home for Rodius Severus and Domitia Pacata, with Rodius's age and date of death recorded as provincial year 256 (likely late 3rd century AD).

EDCS-24400460.translation: Marcus Areius Victor, son of Areius, priest of the Lord Holy Saturn, together with Antonia Victoria and Aelia Valentina and their sons, willingly fulfilled his vow in provincial year 170.
EDCS-24400460.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Areius Victor, a priest of Saturn, along with his family, dated to provincial year 170 (likely 148/149 AD).

EDCS-24400469.translation: Lucius Antistius Gallus, son of Caius, of the tribe Quirina, priest, dedicated the sacred offering. During the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus, in provincial year 11.
EDCS-24400469.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sacred offering by Lucius Antistius Gallus, a priest, dated to the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus and provincial year 11 (date uncertain without context of consuls and province).

EDCS-24400471.translation: Quintus Munatius Urbanus, priest, together with Arrenia Ancilla, his wife, willingly fulfilled his vow in provincial year 207. Quintus Munatius.
EDCS-24400471.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Quintus Munatius Urbanus, a priest, and his wife Arrenia Ancilla, dated to provincial year 207 (likely mid-3rd century AD).

EDCS-24400475.translation: To Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of the divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, consul for the 5th time, imperator for the 6th time, father of the country, by decree of the decurions, at public expense.
EDCS-24400475.summary: This inscription records a public dedication made by decree of the decurions to Emperor Trajan, detailing his titles and powers during his reign, likely around 108 AD.

EDCS-24400482.translation: By command of the holy goddess Caelestis, Gaius Julius Hospes willingly fulfilled his vow to Mercury Augustus, in provincial year 197.
EDCS-24400482.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gaius Julius Hospes to Mercury Augustus, at the command of the goddess Caelestis, dated to provincial year 197 (likely late 2nd or early 3rd century AD).

EDCS-24400483.translation: To the Holy Lord God Saturn and to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Capitolinus, Gaius Iulius Hospes willingly paid his vow in the year 195.
EDCS-24400483.summary: This is a dedication to the gods Saturn and Jupiter by Gaius Iulius Hospes, who paid a vow in the consular year 195.

EDCS-24400484.text_edition: \n o et diis patris et hospitibus diis Mauricis et Genio loci Marcus Cornelius Octavianus \n
EDCS-24400484.translation: To Jupiter and the gods of his father, and to the guest-gods of the Moors, and to the Genius of the place, Marcus Cornelius Octavianus [dedicated this].
EDCS-24400484.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, the guest-gods of the Moors, and the Genius loci by Marcus Cornelius Octavianus.

EDCS-24400863.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, baths for the citizens of the municipium Aelius Choba were constructed at public expense, dedicated by Lucius Aiedius Victor, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, and Marcus Aemilius Honoratus, son of [father's name], of the Arnensis tribe, the two duovirs, in the 157th year of the province.
EDCS-24400863.summary: This is a dedication of public baths constructed for the municipium Aelius Choba, commissioned by duovirs Lucius Aiedius Victor and Marcus Aemilius Honoratus during the reign of Septimius Severus, in the 157th year of the province.

EDCS-24500083.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, father of the country, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the [number] time, imperator for the fourth time, proconsul. Titus FP Fronsusuia Martialis.
EDCS-24500083.summary: This fragmentary inscription records titles of Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus, likely part of a larger dedication or monument.

EDCS-24500084.translation: [dedicated] to the divine Severus Pius, father of [Marcus Aurelius] Antoninus Augustus.
EDCS-24500084.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records a dedication to the deified Emperor Severus, possibly as father of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus.

EDCS-24500089.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus the Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 10th time, imperator for the 6th time, consul for the 4th time, proconsul. Septimius Flaviusanuss, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Mauretania Sitifensis, most devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-24500089.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine by Septimius Flavianus, governor of Mauretania Sitifensis, expressing devotion to the emperor's divine majesty.

EDCS-24500098.translation: Publius Aelius Peregrinus, procurator of the emperors.
EDCS-24500098.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Publius Aelius Peregrinus as an imperial procurator.

EDCS-24500104.translation: The piety and sorrow of Victoris Biarchus, the father, for Victor, his most sweet son, who lived 5 years, 5 months, 13 days, and died on the Kalends of October in the 298th year of the province.
EDCS-24500104.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Victor, the young son of Victoris Biarchus, recording his lifespan and date of death in the 298th year of the province.
