EDCS-24500105.translation: Publius Aelius Saturninus, son of Publius, of the Papiria tribe, having held all the offices, lived 52 years. Here lies Iulia Victoria [who placed this] for her dearest husband, in the 155th year of the province.
EDCS-24500105.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Saturninus, who lived 52 years and held public office, with a dedication by his wife Iulia Victoria in the 155th year of the province.

EDCS-24500113.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Marcus Damatius Urbanus, perfectly educated in the studies of the liberal arts and literature, endowed with excellent eloquence, lived 22 years and 7 days. He lies here on the 9th day before the Kalends of October in the 150th year of the province. Marcus Damatius Felix, his devoted father, made this.
EDCS-24500113.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Damatius Urbanus, who died young but was highly educated, erected by his devoted father in the 150th year of the province.

EDCS-24500114.translation:
In memory of Praetorianus, most beloved son, most ingenious notary, who lived 17 years, 8 months, 17 days. Died in Rome on the 18th day before the Kalends of November in the 186th year of the province.
In memory of Prima, most beloved daughter, who lived 8 years, 5 months. Died on the 5th day before the Kalends of September in the 187th year of the province.
EDCS-24500114.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating two children, Praetorianus and Prima, who died in different years of the province.

EDCS-24500153.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Quintus Caecilius Septiminus lived 72 years, 3 months. Quintus Caecilius Fortunatus [erected this] to his kind father in the 220th year of the province.
EDCS-24500153.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Quintus Caecilius Fortunatus to his father, Quintus Caecilius Septiminus, who died in the 220th year of the province.

EDCS-24700572.translation: To the Salutary Divine Powers. Aurelius Exoratus, decurion of the ala Part(h)orum and prefect of the cohort Sardorum Severianae.
EDCS-24700572.summary: This is an inscription honoring Aurelius Exoratus, a decurion and prefect in the Roman army, dedicated to the divine powers.

EDCS-24700624.inscription_type: carmina, inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sacri, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-24700624.translation: To the holy martyrs, Colonus, innocent, happily fulfilled his vows with his dear wife. Justus and Decurius are buried here together, who, having confessed well, overcame evil weapons; as victors they merited the crown of Christ.
EDCS-24700624.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription commemorating Justus and Decurius, martyrs who suffered persecution but were victorious, likely dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century.

EDCS-24700630.translation: In peace, Marcus Calvari, aged three. Marcus Arimanus Aus, with his son Adiutor, made this memorial in the 198th year of the province.
EDCS-24700630.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Calvari, who died at age three, erected by his father Marcus Arimanus Aus in the 198th year of the province.

EDCS-24700816.translation: [Son] of Istalis. To his most excellent wife and for himself while he was still alive. Iulia Honorata lived 42 years. She is buried here on the 7th day before the Kalends of March in the 177th year of the province.
EDCS-24700816.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Honorata, who died at age 42 in the 177th year of the province, and also includes a dedication for her husband made during his lifetime.

EDCS-24700825.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. To Quintus Licinius Saturninus, his most beloved husband, who lived 62 years. Satricanian Saturnina made [this] with whom she lived 27 years, in the 203rd year of the province. Patrician.
EDCS-24700825.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Satricanian Saturnina to her husband Quintus Licinius Saturninus, who died in the 203rd year of the province, and notes their 27 years of marriage.

EDCS-24700844.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Scantius Faustus lived 25 years. Died in the 282nd year of the province.
EDCS-24700844.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Scantius Faustus, who died at age 25 in the 282nd year of the province.

EDCS-24900490.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Invictus Pius Felix Augustus built the walls of the castellum Dianense through its colonists in the 195th year of the province.
EDCS-24900490.summary: This is a building inscription recording Emperor Alexander Severus's construction of the walls of Castellum Dianense in the 195th year of the province.

EDCS-24900491.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Commodo Antonino Augusto Pio Felici Sarmatico Germanico maximo Brittanico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XV imperatori VIII consuli VI patri patriae indulgentissimo principrincipi divi Marci Antonini Pii filio divi Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti <p=F>er patronum splendidissimarum colon<i=M>arum procuratorem Augusti coloni domini nostri posuerunt \n
EDCS-24900491.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius Felix, Victor of Sarmatia, Germanicus Maximus, the Great, Brit(annicus), Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, Imperator for the 8th time, Consul for the 6th time, Father of the Fatherland, most indulgent prince, son of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius, grandson of the divine Pius, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, 4th-generation descendant of the divine Trajanus Parthicus, 5th-generation descendant of the divine Nerva. The colonists of the most splendid coloniae, through their procurator of the August, have erected [this monument] to their lord.
EDCS-24900491.summary: This is a monumental inscription dedicated by colonists to Emperor Commodus, listing his numerous titles and honors, indicating a significant period of his reign.

EDCS-24900566.translation: For the safety and well-being of our lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and his entire Divine Household, this wall was built from the ground up by his devoted colonists of the Castle of Cella, most devotedly to his Divinity, in the 194th year of the province.
EDCS-24900566.summary: This inscription records the construction of a wall for the safety of Emperor Gordian III, built by the colonists of Castellum Cellense in the 194th year of the province.

EDCS-24900572.text_edition: \n Fortunae Hiegiae sic et (A)escolapi sic numinibus Lucius Apronius Pius legatus Augusti pro praetore vvota solvit\n
EDCS-24900572.translation: To Fortuna Hiegia, and likewise to Asclepius, and to the divine powers, Lucius Apronius Pius, imperial legate with praetorian authority, fulfilled his vows.
EDCS-24900572.summary: This inscription records Lucius Apronius Pius, a Roman governor, fulfilling vows to the deities Fortuna Hiegia and Asclepius.

EDCS-24900600.text_edition: \n Felicissimis temporibus ddominis nnostris Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Iuli Philippi Invicti Pii Felicis et Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Iuli Philippi Invicti Pii Felicis Auggustorum et Marciae Otaciliae Severae Augustae aqua fontis quae multo tempore deperierat et cives inopia aquae laborabant instantia Marci Aurelii Athonis Marcelli viri egregii procuratoris Auggustorum rarissimi praesidis nostri patroni municipii innovato opere aquae ductus abundans in fonte est perducta\n
EDCS-24900600.translation: In most felicitous times for our lords Emperors Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Invictus Pius Felix and Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, and for Empress Marcia Otacilia Severa Augusta, the spring water, which had been lost for a long time and caused the citizens to suffer from a lack of water, was restored through the initiative of Marcus Aurelius Athon Marcellus, an outstanding man, procurator of the Emperors, our most distinguished governor and patron of the municipality, by means of renewed work on the aqueduct, and abundant water now flows into the fountain.
EDCS-24900600.summary: This inscription celebrates the restoration of a water supply to a town during the reign of Philip the Arab and his family, thanks to the efforts of Marcus Aurelius Athon Marcellus, a procurator and governor.

EDCS-25001018.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Cai Fontei Cerealis Gaetuliciani aequitis Romani vixit annos III hic situs est\n
EDCS-25001018.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Gaius Fonteius Cerealis Gaetulicianus, a Roman equestrian, lived for 3 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-25001018.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Fonteius Cerealis Gaetulicianus, a Roman equestrian who died at the age of three.

EDCS-25001023.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Secura vixit annis LXXV provinciae CLXVI\n
EDCS-25001023.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Iulia Secura lived for 75 years. Year 166 of the province.
EDCS-25001023.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Secura, who lived for 75 years and dates to the 166th year of the province.

EDCS-25001046.text_edition: \n Dis Salutaribus Mauris Marcus Aurelius Victor vir egregius procurator praeses provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis fontemcial ceria nius \n
EDCS-25001046.translation: To the Salutary Gods of the Moors. Marcus Aurelius Victor, an outstanding man, procurator and governor of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, [restored?] the spring water...
EDCS-25001046.summary: This inscription records Marcus Aurelius Victor, governor of Mauretania Caesariensis, dedicating a restored spring to the Salutary Gods of the Moors.

EDCS-25001072.translation: Sacred to the Holy God Frugifer Augustus. Sextius Victor, decurion and principal of the Numidae tribe, having been admonished by sacred religious duty, restored the temple, together with its area and altars, with the utmost care, and dedicated it with his own money. The work was carried out under the supervision of Gaius Orte(nsius?) Saturninus and Lucius Aemilius, public defenders, as noted above. In the year 208 of the province.
EDCS-25001072.summary: This inscription details the restoration and dedication of a temple to the god Frugifer Augustus, funded by Sextius Victor, a local official, in the year 208 of the province.

EDCS-25001074.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus muros paganicenses Serteitanis per populares suos fecit curante Sallustio Sempronio Victore procuratore suo instantibus Helvio Crescente decurione alae I Claudiae Kapitonianae \n
EDCS-25001074.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus built the walls of Pagnicenses and Sertitani through his representatives, under the supervision of Sallustius Sempronius Victor, his procurator, and with the urging of Helvius Crescens, decurion of the first Ala Claudia Kapitoniana.
EDCS-25001074.summary: This inscription records Emperor Severus Alexander's construction of town walls for Pagnicenses and Sertitani, overseen by his procurator and local military officials.

EDCS-25001077.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius C. F. lived 85 years. Aelia Norica lived 75 years. In the year 172 of the province.
EDCS-25001077.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages at death of Lucius C. F. (85 years) and Aelia Norica (75 years), dating to the 172nd year of the province.

EDCS-25001083.text_edition: \n Nerone Claudio Caesare Augusto Germanico Lucio Antistio Vetere consulibus Kalendis Augustis Quintus Iulius Quinti filius Quirina Secundus legatus pro praetore hospitium fecit cum decurionibus et colonis colonia Iulia Augusta legionis VII Tupusuctu sibi liberis posterisque suis eosque patrocinio suo tuendos recepit agentibus legatis Quinto Caecilio Quinti filio Palatina Firmano Marco Pomponio Marci filio Quirina Vindice\n
EDCS-25001083.translation: During the consulship of Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus and Lucius Antistius Vetus, on the Kalends of August. Quintus Iulius Quinti filius Quirina Secundus, legate with proconsular authority, established hospitality with the decurions and colonists of the colony Iulia Augusta of the VII Legion at Tupusuctu for himself, his children, and his descendants, and undertook to protect them with his patronage. The agents were Quinto Caecilio Quinti filio Palatina Firmano and Marcus Pomponio Marci filio Quirina Vindice.
EDCS-25001083.summary: This inscription records Quintus Iulius Secundus, a Roman legate, establishing a client-patron relationship with the colony and legion stationed at Tupusuctu for the benefit of himself and his descendants.

EDCS-25001087.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Lucius Tadius Nivalis, son of Marcus, of the Arnensian tribe, who lived 72 years, having completed all honors, is buried here.
EDCS-25001087.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Tadius Nivalis, who lived 72 years and had completed all honors, dated to the 150th year of the province.

EDCS-25001129.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Minucius Fundanus, son of Lucius, lived 65 years.
EDCS-25001129.summary: This is a tombstone for Gaius Minucius Fundanus, who lived 65 years, dated to the 209th year of the province.

EDCS-25100022.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni ceterisque diis immortalibus gratum referens quod coadunatis secum militibus ddominorum nnostrorum Invictissimorum Auggustorum tam ex parte Caesariensi quam etiam de Sitifensi adgressus Quinquegentaneos rebelles caesos multos etiam et vivos adprehensos sed et praedas actas repressa desperatione eorum victoriam reportaverit Aurelius Litua vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis\n
EDCS-25100022.translation: Giving thanks to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno, and all the immortal gods, because by assembling soldiers with him from our Invincible and August Lords, both from the Caesariensian part and also from Sitifensian, he attacked the rebellious Quinquegentani, having killed many and captured others alive, and also seized their plunder, and having suppressed their desperation, he brought back victory. Aurelius Litua, a most perfect man, governor of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis.
EDCS-25100022.summary: This is a dedication by Aurelius Litua, governor of Mauretania Caesariensis, thanking the gods for a military victory against the Quinquegentani rebels.

EDCS-25100026.summary: This is a partial inscription dedicated to Emperor Nerva, likely part of a larger monument.

EDCS-25100035.translation: Gaius Aufidius Honoratus, son of Lucius, of the Arnensian tribe, adorned with the public equestrian rank, councilor of the colony, and his uncle and stepfather, and Sellia Satura, daughter of Quintus, his mother, set up this statue for their most devoted son, and dedicated it, on the 9th day before the Kalends of January, in the consulship of Titus Sextius Lateranus and Gaius Cuspius Rufinus, in the 158th year of the province, and for the dedication to the citizen, distributions were made from the most sacred order.
EDCS-25100035.summary: This is a dedication of a statue and distribution of gifts by a stepfather/uncle and mother for their son, Gaius Aufidius Honoratus, a local official, in the 158th year of the province.

EDCS-25100089.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Arabico Adiabenico Parthico pontifici maximo Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurellio sic Antonino et Publio Septimi[o Getae nobilissimo] Caesari Auggustis turrim ruina lapsam ex praecepto Publi Aeli Peregrini viri egregii procuratoris Auggustorum Rusaditani restituerunt\n
EDCS-25100089.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus, Pontifex Maximus, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurellius Antoninus and to the most noble Emperor Caesar Publius Septimius Geta Augustus, they restored the tower that had collapsed from ruin, by order of Publius Aelius Peregrinus, an outstanding man, procurator of the Augusti at Rusadida.
EDCS-25100089.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a collapsed tower by order of the procurator Publius Aelius Peregrinus, mentioning the reigning emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta.

EDCS-25100112.translation: In honor of his brother. Sapidae Ziarur, sons of Messi Sapidionis. Messia Castula, his sister, [daughter] of the duumvir.
EDCS-25100112.summary: This is a dedication honoring a brother, likely related to the duumvirate of the province in the 188th year of the province.

EDCS-25300012.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Herennius Donatus lived [3] years and his son He[3]a[3]us lived 10[3] years.
EDCS-25300012.summary: This is a tombstone for Herennius Donatus and his son, dated to the 220th year of the province.

EDCS-25300013.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Saturnina. Iulius Cutaius lived 60 years, in the 202nd year of the province. Iulia Maxima. Iulius Tuentius.
EDCS-25300013.summary: This tombstone records the death of Iulius Cutaius at 60 years old in the 202nd year of the province, with other individuals named.

EDCS-25300020.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Clodia Tacamtessa lived twenty years, 2 months, and 5 days. Clodius Mesentius [placed this] for his most sweet daughter.
EDCS-25300020.summary: This is a tombstone for Clodia Tacamtessa, who died at 20 years, 2 months, and 5 days, with her father Clodius Mesentius named, dated to the 220th year of the province.

EDCS-25300023.translation: To Aelia, dearest to her son, he made this.
EDCS-25300023.summary: A son dedicated this tombstone to his beloved Aelia, specifying the road mileage of 211, possibly for administrative purposes.

EDCS-25300280.translation: The memory of Vitula, faithful in peace.
EDCS-25300280.summary: A simple epitaph commemorating Vitula, a faithful companion.

EDCS-25300295.translation: To Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius and Lucius Septimius Geta, most noble Caesars, Titus Aelius Valentinus dedicated this for the honor of the duumvirate and games.
EDCS-25300295.summary: This inscription records the dedication by Titus Aelius Valentinus to several emperors, likely for his holding the office of duumvir, during the Severan period.

EDCS-25300296.translation: To Apollo, Publius Julius Victor gave and dedicated this.
EDCS-25300296.summary: Publius Julius Victor dedicated an offering to Apollo, noting a provincial distance of 109.

EDCS-25300299.translation: Sacred to Good Health; by response of Hercules, Lucius Pescennius Honoratus, its priest, gave and dedicated this.
EDCS-25300299.summary: Lucius Pescennius Honoratus, as priest, dedicated this sanctuary to Bona Valetudo (Good Health) based on an oracle of Hercules, with a provincial distance of 222.

EDCS-25300310.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Aemilia Primosa, gifted by Dionysius... in the 301st year of the province.
EDCS-25300310.summary: A funerary inscription for Aemilia Primosa, mentioning a donor named Dionysius and dating to the 301st year of the province.

EDCS-25300313.translation: To the spirits of the departed. M. Candidus lived 60 years, 3 months, and 7 days.
EDCS-25300313.summary: A tombstone for M. Candidus, recording his lifespan and dated to the 181st year of the province.

EDCS-25500513.translation: To Victoria Augusta, to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, nephew of the deified Severus Pius Augustus, Aurelius Maximus dedicated this in fulfillment of a vow for the honor of the duumvirate.
EDCS-25500513.summary: Aurelius Maximus dedicated this monument to Emperor Marcus Aurelius and other imperial figures in fulfillment of a vow for his office as duumvir.

EDCS-25500514.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 12th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of his country, built this arch of the harbors... in the duumvirate of Gaius Julius and Publius Sallustius Severus... of the province.
EDCS-25500514.summary: This inscription records the construction of an arch of the harbors by Emperor Hadrian during his reign and the duumvirate of two individuals, specifying its date by provincial year.

EDCS-25500515.translation: To the deified Antoninus, father of the Emperors Antoninus and Verus Augusti.
EDCS-25500515.summary: This inscription honors the deified Antoninus Pius as the father of the reigning emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Verus.

EDCS-25500540.translation: In these lands of Marcus Aurelius Vasefanis, a man of highest rank, he completed the camp for his sons and grandsons who will inhabit it, without expense to anyone, starting on the Nonas of February in the 3rd year of the province.
EDCS-25500540.summary: This inscription records the completion of a camp on private land, likely for a family estate, by Marcus Aurelius Vasefanis, dated to the 3rd provincial year.

EDCS-25500560.translation: To the divine power of the Sirens' waters, Porcius Quintus, decurion of a cavalry wing, from a troop commander, dedicated this in the 203rd year of the province.
EDCS-25500560.summary: This dedication is made to the water deities of the Sirens by Porcius Quintus, a military officer, dated to the 203rd provincial year.

EDCS-25500569.translation: To Eternal Victory, through the divinity of Caesar Antoninus, Marcus Antonius Proculleius dedicated this to honor the office of aedile, for sixty equestrian troops.
EDCS-25500569.summary: This is a dedication to Victory and the deified Emperor Antoninus, made by Marcus Antonius Proculleius for an honorable aedileship, likely related to military units.

EDCS-25500573.translation: To Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, wife of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-25500573.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Sabinia Tranquillina, wife of Emperor Gordian III.

EDCS-25601087.translation: To the Unconquered God, Aulisvaes, [ --- ] Fl[ --- ], prefect of the cavalry wing of scouts from Pomaria, and procurator of our Augustus.
EDCS-25601087.summary: This is a dedication to the Unconquered God Aulisvaes by Fl[---], a military officer serving as prefect and procurator, likely in Roman North Africa.

EDCS-25601094.translation: Valeria made this eternal house for Aurelia [---]acus, who lived 35 years, in the 174th year of the province.
EDCS-25601094.summary: This funerary inscription records the construction of an eternal house by Valeria for her daughter Aurelia [---]acus, who died at 35, dated to the 174th provincial year.

EDCS-25601101.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-25601101.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulius Firmus lived 70 years; his son made him an eternal house in the 160th year of the province.
EDCS-25601101.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Iulius Firmus (70 years) and the construction of his eternal house by his son, dated to the 160th provincial year.

EDCS-25601105.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulius Victor lived [ --- ] years and 10 months; his wife made him an eternal house in the 158th year of the province.
EDCS-25601105.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Iulius Victor (length unknown but at least 10 months) and the construction of his eternal house by his wife, dated to the 158th provincial year.

EDCS-25601107.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-25601107.text_edition: \n Iulia Caecilia vicxit sic annis XLV cui Terensus maritus fekit sic domum aeter(nalem) fecit\n
EDCS-25601107.translation: Iulia Caecilia lived 45 years; her husband Terensus made her this eternal house.
EDCS-25601107.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Iulia Caecilia (45 years) and the construction of her eternal house by her husband Terensus.

EDCS-25601112.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Monina lived 30 years; her grandson made her an eternal house in the 155th year of the province.
EDCS-25601112.summary: This funerary inscription records the life of Iulia Monina (30 years) and the construction of her eternal house by her grandson, dated to the 155th provincial year.

EDCS-25601138.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Dimitreas lived 5[X] years; to whom his sons made an eternal home. Year 165 of the province.
EDCS-25601138.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Dimitreas by his sons, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE, recording the construction of a family tomb.

EDCS-25601143.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordiani Pii Felicis Augusti nostri et Sabiniae Tranquillinae Augustae nostrae coniugis Augusti nostri totaque sic domus divina sic eorum Catellius Rufinus procurator eorum\n
EDCS-25601143.translation: For the health of our Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus and our Augusta Sabinia Tranquillina, wife of our Augustus, and for their entire divine household, Catellius Rufinus, their procurator.
EDCS-25601143.summary: This is a dedication made by Catellius Rufinus, a procurator, for the health of Emperor Gordian III, his wife Tranquillina, and their imperial family, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-25601144.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-25601144.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Massamari, opto of the unit of Spurers, who lived approximately 31 years, was killed on the 10th day before the Kalends of April, along with his parents. Possidia Mavitia erected this monument for her most beloved husband. Year 243 of the province.
EDCS-25601144.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Aurelius Massamari, a soldier of the Spurers unit who died in battle with his parents, erected by his wife Possidia Mavitia, dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-25601148.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-25601148.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aelius Deliusus, who lived approximately 70 years, departed on the 12th day before the Kalends of June. Aelius Iunior, his dearest son, and his parents established the Romulan home. Year 305 of the province.
EDCS-25601148.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aelius Deliusus and the establishment of his tomb, the Romulan home, by his son Aelius Iunior and parents, dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-25601160.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Secunda, who lived approximately 90 years, departed on the Ides of June. Year 100 of the province.
EDCS-25601160.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Secunda, who lived to approximately 90 years of age, dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-25601162.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-25601162.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Seppius Marcellus, who lived approximately 40 years, departed on the 10th day before the Kalends of December. Seppius Secundus, his brother, established the Romulan home for his dearest and well-deserving brother. Year 277 of the province.
EDCS-25601162.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Seppius Marcellus, who lived approximately 40 years, and the construction of his tomb, the Romulan home, by his brother Seppius Secundus, dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-25601164.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-25601164.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Cianus Vadanus, who lived approximately 70 years, departed on the 10th day before the Kalends of December. His sons Victor Fabacius and Maximus established the Romulan home. Year 290 of the province.
EDCS-25601164.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Cianus Vadanus, who died at approximately 70 years of age, and records the construction of his tomb, the Romulan home, by his sons Victor Fabacius and Maximus, dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-25601583.translation: In memory of Maecius Rusticus, who preceded [us] in the Lord's peace and lived 60 years. Maecius Victor made [this]. Year 306 of the province.
EDCS-25601583.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Maecius Rusticus, who lived 60 years, and was made by Maecius Victor, likely from the 4th century CE, with a Christian connotation.

EDCS-25601585.translation: To the god Mercury. Lucius Acilius Glabrio fulfills his vow willingly and deservedly. Year 111 of the province.
EDCS-25601585.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Mercury by Lucius Acilius Glabrio, dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-25601598.translation: In memory of Honoratus, who lived approximately 20 years, departed in peace. Year 300 of the province.
EDCS-25601598.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Honoratus, who died at approximately 20 years of age and in peace, likely dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-25601617.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Parthicus Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, acclaimed Imperator for the 11th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, by the equestrian ala I Augusta Parthica Antoniniana.
EDCS-25601617.summary: This is a dedication by the equestrian ala I Augusta Parthica Antoniniana to Emperor Septimius Severus, likely dating to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-25601618.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, [son of the] most noble Caesar Geta, [brother], holding tribunician power for the 4th time, Consul, Proconsul, by the equestrian ala I Augusta Parthica Antoniniana.
EDCS-25601618.summary: This is a dedication by the equestrian ala I Augusta Parthica Antoniniana to Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Antoninus), likely dating to the early part of his sole reign, around 212-213 AD.

EDCS-25601630.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Valerius Restutus erected [this] to his most loving father, who lived for 53 years and passed away on the 4th day before the Ides of September in the 296th year of the province.
EDCS-25601630.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of a father at age 53, set up by his son Valerius Restutus, and dated to 296 AUC (After the provincial era, likely 212/213 AD).

EDCS-25601640.translation: In memory. Aurelia Saturnina lived for 65 years and passed away on the 3rd day before the Nones of November. Her father and sons erected this in the 305th year of the province.
EDCS-25601640.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Aurelia Saturnina at age 65, erected by her father and sons, and dated to 305 AUC (After the provincial era, likely 221/222 AD).

EDCS-25601641.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Marcus Caecilius Donatus lived 55 years and 7 months. Aurelia Boroc erected [this] to her husband with a willing heart in the 295th year of the province.
EDCS-25601641.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Marcus Caecilius Donatus at age 55 years and 7 months, erected by his wife Aurelia Boroc, and dated to 295 AUC (After the provincial era, likely 211/212 AD).

EDCS-25601645.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Lucius Crepereius Secundinus lived 80 years and passed away on the 4th day before the Kalends of March. His children erected [this] to their most sweet father in the 284th year of the province.
EDCS-25601645.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Lucius Crepereius Secundinus at age 80, erected by his children, and dated to 284 AUC (After the provincial era, likely 200/201 AD).

EDCS-25601646.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. To Crepereia Prima, their most sweet mother, who lived 65 years and passed away on the 10th day before the Kalends of March. Her sons and grandsons erected [this] in the 294th year of the province.
EDCS-25601646.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Crepereia Prima at age 65, erected by her sons and grandsons, and dated to 294 AUC (After the provincial era, likely 210/211 AD).

EDCS-25601651.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Florentia Monnina, innocent, lived 4 years and passed away on the 7th day before the Ides of October in the 293rd year of the province.
EDCS-25601651.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of a young child, Florentia Monnina, at age 4, and dated to 293 AUC (After the provincial era, likely 209/210 AD).

EDCS-25601652.translation: Sacred to the departed gods. Fuscia Aemilia, who lived 26 years and passed away on the 10th day before the Kalends of October. Her father erected [this] in the 263rd year of the province.
EDCS-25601652.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Fuscia Aemilia at age 26, erected by her father, and dated to 263 AUC (After the provincial era, likely 179/180 AD).

EDCS-25601658.translation: Julius Victor, most beloved father, lived [X] years, in the 285th year of the province.
EDCS-25601658.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the death of Julius Victor, with his age partially lost, erected by his children, and dated to 285 AUC (After the provincial era, likely 201/202 AD).

EDCS-25601675.translation: To Secundus Capito, dearest father, you lived 13 years, you departed on the 17th day of the month [blank] in the 266th year of the province.
EDCS-25601675.summary: This inscription records the death of a father, Secundus Capito, who lived for an unknown number of years and died in the 266th provincial year.

EDCS-25601679.translation: In memory of Titus Felix, he lived 4 years and departed on the 11th day before the Kalends of February in the 311th year of the province.
EDCS-25601679.summary: This inscription records the death of Titus Felix, who died at the age of 4 in the 311th provincial year.

EDCS-25601680.translation: In memory of Ulpius Serenus, who was also called Macca, he lived 40 years and departed on the Nones of June; for my dearest husband, I made [this] in the 288th year of the province.
EDCS-25601680.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Ulpius Serenus (also known as Macca), set up by his wife, dating to the 288th provincial year.

EDCS-25601682.translation: He departed on the 7th day before the Kalends of [blank] in the 279th year of the province. His sons set [this] up for their dearest elder father.
EDCS-25601682.summary: This inscription records the death of a father, who died in the 279th provincial year, and was honored by his sons.

EDCS-25601683.translation: [In memory?] of Petrus Pecuarius, he lived 25 years, he departed the day before the Ides of December in the 308th year of the province.
EDCS-25601683.summary: This inscription records the death of Petrus Pecuarius, who lived 25 years and died in the 308th provincial year.

EDCS-25601697.translation: To the Holy God Aulisua, Flavius Cassianus, prefect of the troop of scouts of the Pomarienses, [dedicated this, perhaps in the Severian period].
EDCS-25601697.summary: This dedication to a local deity, Aulisua, was made by Flavius Cassianus, a prefect of auxiliary scouts, likely during the Severan period.

EDCS-25900039.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adizabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, and to the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus' son, the deified Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus' grandson, the deified Antoninus Pius' great-grandson, the deified Hadrian's great-great-grandson, the deified Trajan Parthicus' great-great-great-grandson, the deified Nerva's great-great-great-great-grandson, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and to Lucius Septimius Geta nobilissimus Caesar, from public funds the colony Nerviana Augusta Martialis of the veterans of Sitifis [erected this] from Sitifi [for] 1 mile.
EDCS-25900039.summary: This dedication likely marks the construction or repair of a road by the colony of Sitifis, naming multiple Severan emperors and members of the imperial family and indicating a distance of 1 mile.

EDCS-26000951.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, [this marker stands] 3 miles from Sitifi.
EDCS-26000951.summary: This milestone records a distance of 3 miles from Sitifi and is dedicated to Emperor Hadrian during his reign.

EDCS-26000959.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, [this marker stands] 6 miles from Sitifi.
EDCS-26000959.summary: This milestone records a distance of 6 miles from Sitifi and is dedicated to Emperor Hadrian during his reign.

EDCS-26000960.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus pater patriae pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III imperator IIII consul II proconsul miliaria restituit per Cnaeum Nunnium Martialem procuratorem suum a Sitifi milia passuum VI\n
EDCS-26000960.translation: The Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, father of the country, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 3rd time, imperator for the 4th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, restored the milestones through his procurator Gnaeus Nunninius Martialis, from Sitifi 6 miles.
EDCS-26000960.summary: This inscription records the restoration of milestones by Emperor Septimius Severus, as overseen by his procurator, indicating a distance of 6 miles from Sitifi.

EDCS-26100143.text_edition: \n Imperatore Caesare Marco Aurelio Alexandro Pio Felice Augusto pontifice maximo patre patriae? consule proconsule tribuniciae potestatis miliaria nova posita per Aelium Decrianum virum perfectissimum procuratorem suum ab Aras sic milia passuum VI\n
EDCS-26100143.translation: Under Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, father of the country, consul, proconsul, with tribunician power, new milestones were placed by Aelius Decrianus, his perfectissimus (rank), his procurator, from Aras, six Roman miles.
EDCS-26100143.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Alexander Severus, recording the placement of new milestones by his procurator Aelius Decrianus.

EDCS-26100173.text_edition: \n divi Magni Antonini filius divi Severi nepos miliaria nova posuit per Titum Aelium Decrianum procuratorem suum milia passuum VI\n
EDCS-26100173.translation: The son of the divine Antoninus, grandson of the divine Severus, placed new milestones by Titus Aelius Decrianus, his procurator, for six Roman miles.
EDCS-26100173.summary: This milestone inscription, likely referring to Caracalla, records the placement of new milestones by his procurator Titus Aelius Decrianus.

EDCS-26100175.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus and Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus established new milestones by Titus Aelius Decrianus, their procurator.
EDCS-26100175.summary: This damaged inscription records the establishment of new milestones by Emperors Macrinus and his son Diadumenianus, overseen by their procurator Titus Aelius Decrianus.

EDCS-26100179.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius [Severus] [Alexander] Pius Felix Augustus pater patriae consul divi Magni Antonini filius divi Severi nepos miliaria nova posuit per Titum Aelium Decrianum procuratorem suum a numerum sic Severianum sic [Alexan][drianum] sic Syrorum milia passuum II\n
EDCS-26100179.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, father of the country, consul, son of the divine Antoninus, grandson of the divine Severus, placed new milestones by Titus Aelius Decrianus, his procurator, from the Severan standard, the Alexandrian standard, the Syrian standard, for two Roman miles.
EDCS-26100179.summary: This milestone inscription from the reign of Alexander Severus records the placement of new milestones by his procurator Titus Aelius Decrianus, indicating distances relative to specific reference points.

EDCS-26100180.translation: [Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander] Pius Felix Augustus, father of the country, consul, son of the divine Antoninus, grandson of the divine Severus, placed new milestones by Titus Aelius Decrianus, his procurator, from the Severan standard, the Alexandrian standard, the Syrian standard, for three Roman miles.
EDCS-26100180.summary: This partially restored milestone inscription from the reign of Alexander Severus records the placement of new milestones by his procurator Titus Aelius Decrianus, noting distances from various standards.

EDCS-26100181.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus [Alexander] Pius Felix Augustus miliaria posuit per Publium Flavium Clementem procuratorem suum a numero Syrorum Pomariam milia passuum XXVIII Sigam milia passuum XXXVII\n
EDCS-26100181.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus placed milestones by Publius Flavius Clemens, his procurator, from the Syrian standard Pomaria for twenty-eight Roman miles, from Sigam for thirty-seven Roman miles.
EDCS-26100181.summary: This milestone inscription from the reign of Alexander Severus records the placement of milestones by his procurator Publius Flavius Clemens, indicating distances to two locations.

EDCS-26100383.summary: This fragment appears to be the beginning of an inscription dedicated to Emperor Septimius Severus, likely a dedication or honorific.

EDCS-26100399.translation: To the memory of Aelia Secundula. Indeed, we have already sent many worthy provisions for her funeral. Furthermore, we decided to add a stone table for Secundula, the mother, on which, remembering her great deeds, while food and covered coverings are placed, the wound gnawing fiercely in our chest may be healed. Freely we tell stories while we render the late hour. The chaste, good old mother sleeps. Oh nurse, you lie here, always sober. She lived 75 years, in the 256th year of the province. Statulenia Iulia made [this] for her dearest mother.
EDCS-26100399.summary: This epitaph records the life and death of Aelia Secundula, who lived 75 years and died in the 256th year of the province, with a memorial table erected by her daughter Statulenia Iulia.

EDCS-26100400.translation: Aurelius Donatus lived 85 years, in the 163rd year of the province.
EDCS-26100400.summary: This tombstone records the life of Aurelius Donatus, who lived 85 years and died in the 163rd year of the province.

EDCS-26100403.translation: Flavius Viator lived 85 years, in the 225th year of the province.
EDCS-26100403.summary: This tombstone records the life of Flavius Viator, who lived 85 years and died in the 225th year of the province.

EDCS-26100421.translation: In memory of Dativa, also called Saguntina, devout and saintly, her mother Privata placed [this monument] on the 9th day before the Kalends of June, in the 310th year of the province.
EDCS-26100421.summary: A memorial erected by a mother, Privata, for her daughter Dativa (Saguntina) in the 310th year of the province, likely marking a burial site.

EDCS-26100422.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-26100422.translation: To Ianuaria, handmaid of Christ. She lived in God for nearly 60 years and made this table for herself. The holy table of the saints. Dedicated on the 8th day before the Kalends of July, with Avianus Crescens officiating, in the 285th year of the province.
EDCS-26100422.summary: A Christian funerary inscription for Ianuaria, a 'handmaid of Christ', who built her own tombstone ('table') and is dated to the 285th year of the province.

EDCS-26100424.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-26100424.translation: In memory of Flora, a good wife. Her husband Quetus placed this everlasting table. She lived 60 years, died on the 8th day before the Kalends of March, in the 310th year of the province.
EDCS-26100424.summary: A funerary monument set up by a husband, Quetus, for his wife Flora, recording her age and death date in the 310th year of the province.

EDCS-26100425.translation: In memory of Julius Fortunus, good and most innocent. He lived 21 years. In the 283rd year of the province.
EDCS-26100425.summary: A funerary inscription for Julius Fortunus, recording his age and the year of the province, dated to 283.

EDCS-26100426.translation: In memory of Julius Ursicinus. He lived 11 years. In the 303rd year of the province.
EDCS-26100426.summary: A funerary inscription for Julius Ursicinus, recording his age and the year of the province, dated to 303.

EDCS-26100430.translation: Victorinus lived [incomplete]. In the 310th year of the province.
EDCS-26100430.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription for Victorinus, recording his name and the year of the province, dated to 310.

EDCS-26200309.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-26200309.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 4th time, Father of his Country. Quintus Pomponius Saturninus and Titus Pomponius Suavis placed and dedicated [this] at their own expense, according to their promise, because they were admitted into the number of citizens by a decree of the council.
EDCS-26200309.summary: A dedication by Quintus Pomponius Saturninus and Titus Pomponius Suavis to Emperor Antoninus Pius, commemorating their admission into citizenship, likely erected in the 15th year of the emperor's tribunician power (c. 152 AD).

EDCS-26200334.translation: To Marcus Domitius Gentianus, son of Marcus, of the Quirina tribe, honored as a public dignitary by the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, the Laurentine Flaminius of our Augustus, Gaius Domitius Donatus, his freedman and procurator, [erected this] on account of his merits.
EDCS-26200334.summary: This inscription is a dedication honoring Marcus Domitius Gentianus, a distinguished public official and priest, by his freedman and procurator.

EDCS-26200662.translation: The temple to the Genius of Novaria Augustus, corrupted and deposited to its foundations, was restored and dedicated by Titus Coelius Martialis, an outstanding man and patron, and by the citizens of Novaria at their own expense, under the supervision of Marcus Valerius Gentius, on the 17th day before the Kalends of February in the 205th year of the province.
EDCS-26200662.summary: This inscription records the restoration and dedication of a temple to the Genius of Novaria Augustus by local citizens and patrons.

EDCS-26200663.translation: To the Genius of Novaria Augustus, Marcus Valerius Gentius fulfilled his vow willingly and with pleasure, by decree of the decurions, on the 17th day before the Kalends of February in the 205th year of the province.
EDCS-26200663.summary: This inscription records an individual's fulfillment of a vow to the Genius of Novaria Augustus, decreed by the local council.

EDCS-26200668.translation: Sacred to Saturninus Augustus. Publius Clodius Felix Bulin, his grandson, a priest, fulfilled his vow and dedicated it willingly and joyfully on the Ides of June in the 223rd year of the province.
EDCS-26200668.summary: A dedication to Saturninus Augustus by Publius Clodius Felix Bulin, a priest, dated to the 223rd year of the province.

EDCS-26200669.translation: Sacred to Saturninus Augustus. Lucius Gargilius Felix, a priest, fulfilled his vow willingly and joyfully, dedicated on the 9th day before the Kalends of June in the 1[--]th year of the province.
EDCS-26200669.summary: A vow fulfillment to Saturninus Augustus by Lucius Gargilius Felix, a priest, with a partially illegible provincial year.

EDCS-26200670.translation: Sacred to Saturninus Augustus. Junius Secundus fulfilled his vow and dedicated it willingly and joyfully on the 8th day before the Ides of [--] in the CC2nd year of the province.
EDCS-26200670.summary: A dedication to Saturninus Augustus by Junius Secundus, dated to the 202nd year of the province.

EDCS-26200671.translation: Sacred to Saturninus Augustus. Lucius Licinius Donatus, priest, fulfilled his vow and dedicated it willingly and joyfully on the 4th day before the Kalends of January in the 183rd year of the province.
EDCS-26200671.summary: A vow fulfillment to Saturninus Augustus by Lucius Licinius Donatus, a priest, dated to the 183rd year of the province.

EDCS-26200674.translation: Sacred to Saturninus Augustus. Marcus Millius Donatus Inanis, priest, fulfilled his vow and dedicated it willingly and joyfully on the 11th day before the Kalends of March in the 225th year of the province.
EDCS-26200674.summary: A dedication to Saturninus Augustus by Marcus Millius Donatus Inanis, a priest, dated to the 225th year of the province.

EDCS-26200675.translation: Sacred to Saturninus Augustus. Marcus Pompeius Felix, priest, fulfilled his vow and dedicated it willingly and joyfully on the Ides of November in the 188th year of the province.
EDCS-26200675.summary: A dedication to Saturninus Augustus by Marcus Pompeius Felix, a priest, dated to the 188th year of the province.

EDCS-26200696.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the dead. Rubrius Baric lived 25 years; Iulia Naberia lived 70 years. Marcus Rubrius SEIISIAI; Marcus Rubrius Ia[--]aris from the 170th year of the province.
EDCS-26200696.summary: A funerary inscription recording the ages of Rubrius Baric and Iulia Naberia, with surviving names and a provincial year.

EDCS-26200705.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-26200705.translation: The table of Publius Aelius Nampulus, who lived 83 years and 4 months, on the day of his burial, the Ides of May, in the 303rd year of the province. His son, Publius Aelius Donatus, made it.
EDCS-26200705.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Publius Aelius Nampulus and commissioned by his son, dated to the 303rd year of the province.

EDCS-26200707.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-26200707.translation: Cerelius Metumus, son, dedicated this table to my father Maxsimus, who lived 80 years, on the 7th day before the Kalends of April in the 292nd year of the province.
EDCS-26200707.summary: A funerary inscription by Cerelius Metumus dedicating a table for his father Maxsimus, who lived 80 years, dated to the 292nd year of the province.

EDCS-26200708.translation: In memory of Lucius Capsidius Loncurus, a most simple man, who lived 45 years. Lucius Caepidius Vicipuitira and Iulia RKNA amieret PF made it on the day of burial, the 8th day before the Kalends of [April?] in the 288th year of the province.
EDCS-26200708.summary: A funerary inscription commemorating Lucius Capsidius Loncurus, who lived 45 years, and commissioned by Lucius Caepidius Vicipuitira and Iulia RKNA amieret PF, dated to the 288th year of the province.

EDCS-26200711.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-26200711.translation: Eternal tombstone. Ianuarius lived 75 years, died on the Kalends of September. This is the eternal house. Lucius T. Cicero made it for him in the 285th year of the province.
EDCS-26200711.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan and burial of Ianuarius and noting that the tomb was constructed by Lucius T. Cicero in the 285th year of the province.

EDCS-26200754.translation: To the perpetual, most victorious, most indulgent Emperor, restorer of the world, Lucius Domitius Aurelianus.
EDCS-26200754.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Emperor Aurelian, praising his victories and his role in restoring the world, likely dating to his reign (270-275 AD).

EDCS-26200755.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Numerianus, the unconquered, pious...
EDCS-26200755.summary: This is a fragmentary dedicatory inscription honoring Emperor Numerian, dating to his reign (283-284 AD).

EDCS-26200791.text_edition: \n qui et Antiochianus equo publico Publio Aelio Peregrino procuratori Auggggustorum\n
EDCS-26200791.translation: ...who was also Antiochianus, to Publius Aelius Peregrinus, procurator of the Augusti, by equestrian rank.
EDCS-26200791.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records an honorific dedication to Publius Aelius Peregrinus, a procurator of the Augusti, possibly mentioning his origin or epithet 'Antiochianus'.

EDCS-26200802.translation: ...made for himself and his parents while alive, on the day of burial, the 3rd day before the Kalends of July in the 221st year of the province.
EDCS-26200802.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the self-dedicated tomb of an individual for himself and his parents, dated to the 3rd day before the Kalends of July in the 221st year of the province.

EDCS-26200804.translation: In memory of Flavius Surus, dispenser of taxes (?). He died in the year [270?] of the province, the 4th day before the Ides of January. Felix the master.
EDCS-26200804.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Flavius Surus, a tax dispenser, who died on January 9th in the approximately 270th year of the province, with Felix the master mentioned.

EDCS-26200810.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Volussius Donatus, son of Gaius Volussius Caecilianus, lived 82 years. In the 261st year of the province.
EDCS-26200810.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Gaius Volussius Donatus, son of Gaius Volussius Caecilianus, who lived 82 years and died in the 261st year of the province.

EDCS-26200814.text_edition: \n Indulgentia novi saeculi Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordiani Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti patris patriae quod antehac angustio spatio cinctum continebatur nunc reparatis ac fotis viribus fiducia pacis optante faciem maioris loci prolatum est Faltonio Restitutiano viro egregio praeside cura Aurelio Felice Collocatum Kalendis Ianuaris\n
EDCS-26200814.translation: By the indulgence of the new era of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, father of the country. What was formerly enclosed by a narrow space has now been enlarged with repaired and reinforced strength, with the confidence of peace. Placed on the Kalends of January by the excellent governor Faltonius Restitutianus, through the care of Aurelius Felix Collocatus.
EDCS-26200814.summary: This inscription records the expansion and improvement of a public space, attributed to Emperor Gordian III and overseen by governor Faltonius Restitutianus and Aurelius Felix Collocatus, dated to January 1st.

EDCS-26200824.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Grania Felicia lived 23 years. Granius Felix [erected this] for his daughter. In the 198th year of the province.
EDCS-26200824.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Grania Felicia at the age of 23, erected by her father Granius Felix, in the 198th year of the province.

EDCS-26200825.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Julius Mustiolus lived 35 years. Gellia Zaba made it for him in the 185th year of the province. [---] for his own [family].
EDCS-26200825.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Gellia Zaba made a tomb for Julius Mustiolus, who lived 35 years, in the 185th year of the province.

EDCS-26200834.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. ... / ... Felix ... / ... of the province 240.
EDCS-26200834.summary: A funerary inscription with much illegible text, possibly mentioning Felix and a provincial date.

EDCS-26300009.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Valerius Licinius made and dedicated this for himself and Aurelia Victorina, his wife, while they were alive. Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Gaius Valerius Successus lived 18 years. Province 230.
EDCS-26300009.summary: A funerary monument erected by Valerius Licinius for himself and his wife, and also recording the life and death of Gaius Valerius Successus, dated to province year 230.

EDCS-26300017.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus Brittanicus maximus securitati provincialium suorum consulens turres novas instituit et veteres refecit opera militum suorum curante Claudio Perpetuo procuratore suo\n
EDCS-26300017.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus Britannicus Maximus, concerned for the security of his provinces, established new towers and repaired old ones, with the work of his soldiers, under the supervision of his procurator Claudius Perpetuus.
EDCS-26300017.summary: An official inscription recording Emperor Commodus's actions to improve provincial defenses by building and repairing towers, overseen by his procurator Claudius Perpetuus.

EDCS-26300024.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Inalnia Saturnina lived 40 years, 6 months, and 15 days. Valerius, her husband, made this for her because of her devotion, together with his sons. In the year of the province 231.
EDCS-26300024.summary: A funerary inscription for Inalnia Saturnina, erected by her husband Valerius and their sons, dated to province year 231.

EDCS-26300026.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Geniisque diis immortalibus victoriisque ddominorum nnostrorum Invictorum Marcus Aurelius Vitalis vir egregius patronus provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis Ulpius Castus decurio alae Thracum ob barbaros caesos ac fusos votum solverunt libentes animo Idus Augustas anno provinciae CC et XV\n
EDCS-26300026.translation: Marcus Aurelius Vitalis, a distinguished man and patron of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, and Ulpius Castus, a decurion of the Thracian ala, paid their vows willingly with grateful hearts to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the immortal gods, and the victorious spirits of our unconquered lords, for barbarians slain and routed. The Ides of August, in the year of the province 215.
EDCS-26300026.summary: A dedication by two high-ranking military officials to Jupiter and other deities for a victory over barbarians in Mauretania Caesariensis, dated to province year 215.

EDCS-26300028.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, made this.
EDCS-26300028.summary: An official dedication by Emperor Hadrian, listing his titles and powers, indicating he commissioned a public work.

EDCS-26300029.text_edition: \n Imperatoribus Caesaribus Augustis Marco Aurelio Antonino Armeniaco Parthico maximo Medico tribuniciae potestatis cons(uli) III et Lucio Vero Armeniaco Parthico maximo Medico tribunicia potestate <V=X>II consuli III veterani et pagani consistentes aput sic Rapidum murum a fundamentis lapide quadrato exstruxerunt pecunia et sumtu sic omni suo id est veteranorum et paganorum intra eundem eundem murum inhabitantium adiuvante et curante viro egregio Baio Pudente procuratore Augustorum optimo praeside eodemque dedicante \n
EDCS-26300029.translation: Veterans and civilians living near the Rapidum wall, with the help and supervision of the distinguished man Baio Pudens, procurator of the emperors and excellent governor, who also dedicated it, built the wall from its foundations with squared stone, using their own money and resources, in honor of the Emperors Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, victor of Armenia, Parthia, and Media, consul for the 3rd time, and Lucius Verus, victor of Armenia, Parthia, and Media, holding tribunician power for the 17th time, consul for the 3rd time.
EDCS-26300029.summary: An inscription detailing the construction of the Rapidum wall by local veterans and civilians, funded by themselves, in honor of Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, and dedicated by the governor Baio Pudens.

EDCS-26300043.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Vespasiano Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate VI imperatori consuli V des(ignato)? VI patri patriae Titus Flavius nius aedilis IIvir quinquennalis pontifex primus in colonia ex decreto decurionum ob honorem pontificatus epulo dato decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-26300043.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, emperor and designated consul for the 6th time, father of the country. Titus Flavius, aedile for the 2nd time, quinquennial duovir, first priest, from the colony, by decree of the decurions, for the honor of the priesthood, having given a banquet, by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-26300043.summary: An inscription recording a decree by the decurions of a colony honoring Titus Flavius, likely for his priestly office and a banquet he provided, possibly related to Vespasian's reign.

EDCS-26300071.summary: A simple funerary epitaph for Magnia Crescentia.

EDCS-26300391.translation: To Gaius Julius Felix, son of Gaius, of the Quirina tribe, from Rusuccuritanus, prefect, acting as duovir, also quinquennial duovir, flamen, and perpetual augur, Julius Vitalio and Fortunatus, his most indulgent patrons.
EDCS-26300391.summary: A dedication by freedmen (or clients) to their patron, Gaius Julius Felix, who held several important municipal offices.

EDCS-26300392.translation: To Annia Nicene, my most obedient wife, Gaius Julius Felix.
EDCS-26300392.summary: A dedication to a wife from her husband, Gaius Julius Felix.

EDCS-26300398.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Flavitata, wife of Caius Datius, her husband, who lived 47 years, in the 189th year of the province.
EDCS-26300398.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavitata, wife of Caius Datius, dated to the 189th year of the province.

EDCS-26300415.translation: Caius Cornelius Aquila, quaestor, aedile, and now duumvir, happily dedicated to the holy August Celestial gods a temple built from the ground up with his own money, along with his father Cornelius Donatus and his son Victor, in the 174th year of the province.
EDCS-26300415.summary: A dedication of a temple built by Caius Cornelius Aquila, a local magistrate, to the Celestial gods, dated to the 174th year of the province.

EDCS-26300416.translation: Quintus Geminius Renatus and Gaius Attius Plautius Donatus, duumvirs, constructed and dedicated a temple to the most holy August Celestial gods, which had fallen into ruin from age, from the ground up, in the 183rd year of the province.
EDCS-26300416.summary: A dedication of a rebuilt temple to the Celestial gods by two duumvirs, Quintus Geminius Renatus and Gaius Attius Plautius Donatus, dated to the 183rd year of the province.

EDCS-26300417.translation: Gaius Julius Libosus with all his family willingly paid his vow to the August Celestial gods on the eleventh day before the Kalends of July in the 202nd year of the province.
EDCS-26300417.summary: A vow paid by Gaius Julius Libosus and his family to the Celestial gods, dated to the 202nd year of the province.

EDCS-26300418.translation: Lucius Cassius Restutus, former decurion and veteran, perpetual priest of the colony, and his wife Clodia Luciosa made and dedicated a temple with its ornaments to the most holy Good Goddess of Health with their own money, and also gave it as a gift to the state in the 196th year of the province.
EDCS-26300418.summary: A dedication of a temple and its ornaments to the Good Goddess of Health by Lucius Cassius Restutus and his wife Clodia Luciosa, who also donated it to the community, dated to the 196th year of the province.

EDCS-26300438.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. To Claudia Iucunda, her infant, most innocent daughter, dedicated on the Ides of January in the 225th year of the province.
EDCS-26300438.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to an infant daughter, Claudia Iucunda, dated to the 225th year of the province.

EDCS-26300449.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-26300449.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aurelia Rogata, wife of Iulius Calvus Favorinus and mother of Rogatus, placed this tablet of refreshment with offerings for the memory of the living, and dedicated it. Calvus lived 65 years, Rogatus 20 years, Favorinus 44 years, in the 279th year of the province.
EDCS-26300449.summary: A funerary inscription commemorating Aurelia Rogata's husband Iulius Calvus Favorinus and son Rogatus, providing their ages and the year of the province as 279.

EDCS-26500060.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the Emperor [1] Augusti and of the camps, Gaius Julius Turranicus, in honor of his aedileship, in the 170th year of the province.
EDCS-26500060.summary: An honorary inscription for Julia Augusta by Gaius Julius Turranicus in honor of his aedileship, dated to the 170th year of the province.

EDCS-26500062.translation: To the Emperor, our perpetual lord, Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maxentius, Pious, Happy, Victorious, and most glorious Augustus, son of the deified Maximian, son-in-law of the deified Maximian, most fortunate of Emperors of the whole world. Valerius Faustus, vir perfectissimus, praeses of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, devoted to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-26500062.summary: An inscription dedicating a province to Emperor Maxentius by Valerius Faustus, the governor of Mauretania Caesariensis, highlighting Maxentius's lineage and titles.

EDCS-26500066.text_edition: \n Tito Licinio Hierocleti viro egregio procuratori Augusti nostri praesidi provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis praesidi provinciae Sardiniae praefecto legionis secundae Parthicae Severianae [Alexandrianae] vice legati procuratori hereditatium tribuno cohortis octavae praetoriae piae vindiciis sic Severianae [Alexandrianae] praeposito equitum itemque peditum iuniorum Maurorum iure gladii tribuno cohortis undecimae urbanae Severianae [Alexandrianae] primipilum sic bis et Claudiae Nervianae coniugi eius et Liciniis Hierocleti Hierocliae Paulinae et Axiae filiis eorum Marcus Aelius Saturninus veteranus ex decurione alario patronis dignissimis\n
EDCS-26500066.translation: To Titus Licinius Hierocles, an outstanding man, procurator of our Augustus, governor of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, governor of the province of Sardinia, prefect of the Severian (Alexandrian) Second Parthian Legion, acting legate, procurator of inheritances, tribune of the eighth praetorian cohort, piously vindicating the Severian (Alexandrian) claims, prefect of junior Mauran cavalry and infantry with the power of life and death, tribune of the Severian (Alexandrian) Eleventh Urban Cohort, twice a primipilar officer, and to his wife Claudia Nerviana, and to their sons Licinius Hierocles, Hieroclia Paulina, and Axia, Marcus Aelius Saturninus, a veteran, formerly decurion of the auxiliary cavalry, (dedicated this) to his most worthy patrons.
EDCS-26500066.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a veteran to his patrons, including Titus Licinius Hierocles, who held numerous high-ranking military and administrative posts, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-26500067.text_edition: \n Caio Octavio Pudenti Caesio Honorato procuratori Auggustorum provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis procuratori a censibus abstinentiss[imo ------\n
EDCS-26500067.translation: To Gaius Octavius Pudentus Caesius Honoratus, procurator of the Augusti of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, most abstinent procurator of taxes.
EDCS-26500067.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Octavius Pudentus Caesius Honoratus, who served as a procurator for the emperors in Mauretania Caesariensis and was responsible for tax collection, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26500076.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Baiae Hygiae libertae Bai Pudentis procuratoris Augusti Verecundus libertus eiusdem coniugi karissimae fecit vixit annos XXII\n
EDCS-26500076.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Verecundus, freedman of Baiae Pudentis, a procurator of Augustus, made this for his dearest wife Baia Hygia, a freedwoman of the same Baiae Pudentis. She lived 22 years.
EDCS-26500076.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Baia Hygia, wife of the freedman Verecundus, and daughter of Baiae Pudentis, a procurator of Augustus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26500082.translation: Previously, the place had been arranged for the sister in such a way that when the time came for others, places would be made ready, so that after the place was destroyed and both the husband and wife reclaimed, the place that had been freed would be enclosed again. After a very long time, this was not done. Now, Flavius Honoratus, son of the first place holder, a sesquiplicarius of the office of the province of Sitifensis, made this for his most loving father Honorius and mother Saturnina. 303 years of the province.
EDCS-26500082.summary: This inscription records the reallocation of burial plots over time, with the final arrangement made by Flavius Honoratus for his parents, dated to 303 years of the province, likely in North Africa.

EDCS-26500106.text_edition: \n Marco Iulio Iuliano Iuli Theofili decurionis alae Sebastenae Severianae itemque decurionis splendidissimae coloniae Caesariensis equitis Romani filio CTRV provinciae CXCV\n
EDCS-26500106.translation: To Marcus Julius Julianus, son of Julius Theophilus, decurion of the Severian Sebastenian ala, and also decurion of the most splendid colony of Caesariensis, a Roman knight. 175 years of the province.
EDCS-26500106.summary: This inscription commemorates Marcus Julius Julianus, a Roman knight and decurion in both a cavalry unit and a prominent colony, dated to 175 years of the province, likely in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26500139.translation: Fabricated and erected by Marcus Cassius Divicanus, curator, prefect acting for the aediles who dedicated it: Messius Proculus and Caecilius Constans, duovirs, on the Ides of November, in the 149th year of the province, under consuls Fusianus II and Silanus II. Moved from neglected places.
EDCS-26500139.summary: This inscription details the construction and dedication of a public work or building by various officials, including a curator and duovirs, and notes that it was moved, dated to 149 years of the province under consuls Fusianus II and Silanus II, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-26800201.translation: From the estates of Lucius Aelius Caesar. Pottery work by Aristius, Augustalis, under consuls Salvius and Torquatus.
EDCS-26800201.summary: This inscription records pottery production from the estates of Lucius Aelius Caesar, overseen by Aristius, an Augustalis, and dated by the consulship of Salvius and Torquatus, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-26800752.translation: In memory of Ennagus and Sextus. On the Kalends of August, in memory of the most blessed martyrs, namely Rogatus, Maternus, Nasseius, Maxima, whom their parents dedicated. Martyred on the 12th day before the Kalends of November in the 290th year of the province.
EDCS-26800752.summary: This is a memorial inscription for martyrs, likely in North Africa, commemorating Ennagus, Sextus, Rogatus, Maternus, Nasseius, and Maxima, and dating their martyrdom to the 290th year of the province.

EDCS-26800754.translation: [From the time] of Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus.
EDCS-26800754.summary: This inscription fragment commemorates Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus, listing his numerous honorific titles and conquests, likely dating to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-26800757.translation: To the Invincible God Mithras Sol. For the health of our lord Imperator Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus. Aurelius [---]sius, tribune of the cohort of Sardinians, with his men, fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-26800757.summary: This votive inscription is dedicated to the god Sol Invictus Mithras for the health of Emperor Gordian III by Aurelius [---]sius, a tribune of the Sardinian cohort, likely dating to Gordian III's reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-26800787.text_edition: \n Pro salute et victoria et reditu Gordiani Augusti diis immortalibus Livianus procurator\n
EDCS-26800787.translation: For the safety, victory, and return of Emperor Gordian, Livianus, the procurator, dedicated [this] to the immortal gods.
EDCS-26800787.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the immortal gods for the welfare and victories of Emperor Gordian, made by Livianus, a procurator.

EDCS-26800789.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iuno sic Regina sic pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Antoni Gordiani Invicti Pii Felicis Augusti pontifici sic maximi tribuniciae potestatis pater sic patriae imperatoris VII consulis proconsulis Sabiniae Tranquillinae Augustae coniugis Catenio \n
EDCS-26800789.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Queen Juno, and likewise for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, Father of the Fatherland, Imperator seven times, Consul, Proconsul, and for Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, his wife, Catenius [dedicated this].
EDCS-26800789.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, Juno, and for the welfare of Emperor Gordian and his wife Tranquillina, likely made by Catenius.

EDCS-26800790.translation: Sacred to [a deity], Aelius Servandus, decurion and commander of the II Breucorum Cohort (Gordian), placed [this] on the 3rd day before the Kalends of January, when Annio and Papo were consuls, wishing the Emperors many happy years.
EDCS-26800790.summary: This dedication, dated to January 1st, 239 CE, was set up by Aelius Servandus, an officer in the Gordian cohort of Breucorum soldiers, to a deity.

EDCS-26800812.translation: To the Eternal God, a vow which Rogatus, freedman of Sabinus, promised, he made; province 222.
EDCS-26800812.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Aeternus, dedicated by Rogatus, a freedman, recording province 222.

EDCS-26800848.text_edition: \n [Publio Septimio Getae] [Caesari] Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti Parthici Adiabenici pacatoris orbis et fundatoris imperi Romani filio res publica Portumagnensium\n
EDCS-26800848.translation: The community of Portus Magnus [dedicated this] to Publius Septimius Geta Caesar, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, conqueror of Parthia and Adiabene, pacifier of the world and founder of the Roman Empire.
EDCS-26800848.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the community of Portus Magnus to the deceased prince Geta, son of Septimius Severus.

EDCS-26800849.text_edition: \n [Imperatori Caesari] [Publio Septimio] [Getae Pio Fe][lici Augusto] Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis nnostri sic Brittanici [fra][tri] Severi Augusti dei nostri filio res publica Portumagnensium\n
EDCS-26800849.translation: [This was dedicated by] the community of Portus Magnus to the Emperor Caesar Publius Septimius Geta Pius Felix Augustus, son of our Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Britannicus, and brother of Severus Augustus, our divine father.
EDCS-26800849.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the community of Portus Magnus to Emperor Geta, referencing his lineage and relation to Caracalla and Septimius Severus.

EDCS-26800861.translation: Sacred to Juno Sancta Silvana, to Sol, an altar from the ground was dedicated through the efforts of Silumbus Domitianus, dispatcher, by the work of the provincials; province 300.
EDCS-26800861.summary: This is a dedication to Juno Sancta Silvana and Sol, erected by provincials under the supervision of Silumbus Domitianus.

EDCS-26800864.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Publius Aelius Felix, most beloved, who was perfect in the Vivarii of the Bithynians, he lived 30 years. [His] mother [and possibly others] made the altar and inscription; province 200.
EDCS-26800864.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Publius Aelius Felix, who died at age 30, erected by his mother and possibly others.

EDCS-26800871.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Because of the unique affection which Caecilia Romana felt for her husband Marcus Aurelius Processus, she erected this monument within 30 days; he lived 48 years. Province 326.
EDCS-26800871.summary: This tombstone inscription records the dedication by Caecilia Romana for her husband Marcus Aurelius Processus, who lived 48 years.

EDCS-26800883.translation: [...] about 60 [years?] [...] Pudentina, his wife, together with [her] [...] made [this]; province 360.
EDCS-26800883.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Pudentina and likely her husband, possibly commemorating a marriage or tomb, with a record of province 360.

EDCS-26800912.translation: For 7 days. Zoticus... to the most pious... governor of the province.
EDCS-26800912.summary: This inscription likely records a period of service or duty, possibly by Zoticus, who held a position in the province.

EDCS-26801021.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Calidius Iulianus lived 23 years, 4 months, and 214 days. (age marker or epitaph)
EDCS-26801021.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Calidius Iulianus, recording his lifespan and likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26801041.translation: In good memory of Emmartis and her daughter. She lived 18 years, 2 months, and 5 days, and died on the Kalends of May. (epitaph)
EDCS-26801041.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Emmartis and her daughter, detailing their lifespan and date of death.

EDCS-26801044.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Octavia Ianuarua erected this for her most pious parents, Octavio Baricus and Iulia Victorina, and for her dearest brother, Octavio Callius, who lived 25 years, 4 months, and 14 days. (epitaph)
EDCS-26801044.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Octavia Ianuarua to her parents and brother, recording the brother's lifespan.

EDCS-26801046.text_edition: \n Diis patriis et Mauris conservatoribus Aelius Aelianus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Mauretaniae Caesariensis ob prostratam gentem Bavarum Mesegneitisium praedasque omnes ac familias eorum abductas votum solvit\n
EDCS-26801046.translation: Aelius Aelianus, a most accomplished man and governor of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, fulfilled his vow to the ancestral gods and the protecting Mauri, because the Bavarum tribe was defeated, their plunder and all their families abducted.
EDCS-26801046.summary: This inscription records the vow fulfillment by Aelius Aelianus, governor of Mauretania Caesariensis, after a victory against the Bavarum tribe.

EDCS-26801049.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Fausta made this monument for her well-deserving husband, Iulius Saturninus, who lived 45 years. (epitaph)
