EDCS-27100210.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulius Rufinus, erected by his brothers and wife, dating to the provincial year 421.

EDCS-27100211.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Sammata, who lived 25 years. Quintius Felix, her husband, grieving, established this tomb in the 335th year of the province.
EDCS-27100211.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Sammata, established by her grieving husband Quintius Felix, dated to the 335th provincial year.

EDCS-27100212.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Eanuria, cruelly, lived more or less 16 years, departed on the 11th day before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-27100212.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulia Eanuria, recording her age and date of death.

EDCS-27100213.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintius Quintus, cruelly, lived 6 years, departed on the 16th day before the Kalends of January. Quintius Felix, his father, established this tomb for his dearest daughter in the 320th year of the province.
EDCS-27100213.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintius Quintus, established by his father Quintius Felix, dated to the 320th provincial year.

EDCS-27100214.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintia Ianuaria, who lived 70 years, departed on the 3rd day before the Nonas of October. Ianuarius and Victorinus, her sons, established this tomb in the 350th year of the province.
EDCS-27100214.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintia Ianuaria, established by her sons Ianuarius and Victorinus, dated to the 350th provincial year.

EDCS-27100215.translation: Ranius PI, who lived more or less 35 years. Ranius Modare, his brother, grieving, set up this tomb in the year.
EDCS-27100215.summary: A funerary epitaph for Ranius PI, set up by his grieving brother Ranius Modare.

EDCS-27100216.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Seppia Saturnina, dearest daughter, who lived 501 years.
EDCS-27100216.summary: A funerary epitaph for Seppia Saturnina, noting her exceptionally long lifespan.

EDCS-27100217.translation: Table of Gasenis, son of MASERE, lived more or less 73 years and 1 month, in the 413th year of the province.
EDCS-27100217.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gasenis, son of MASERE, recording his age and date, dated to the 413th provincial year.

EDCS-27100218.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Petronius Castus made this for his most pious sons Quintus and Castulus, who lived 6 years and 4 months; they are buried here.
EDCS-27100218.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus and Castulus, made by their father Quintus Petronius Castus.

EDCS-27100219.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Victor Germani, lived.
EDCS-27100219.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Victor Germani.

EDCS-27100590.summary: A short inscription indicating the origin or owner of a workshop.

EDCS-27100642.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a larger text, recording the name of Caius Clodius Successus.

EDCS-27100643.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a larger text, recording the name of Caius Clodius Successus.

EDCS-27100644.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a larger text, recording the name of Caius Clodius Successus.

EDCS-27600107.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, grandson of the deified Gordian, son of the sister of the deified Gordian, the Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul, proconsul, father of the country, holding the twentieth fasces.
EDCS-27600107.summary: This inscription records the full titulature of Emperor Gordian III, likely from the period of his reign (AD 238-244).

EDCS-27600109.summary: This is a dative dedication to Emperor Aurelian, suggesting it was part of a monument or gift.

EDCS-27600110.translation: To the Perpetual Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, the Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, consul for the third time, holding the forty-first fasces.
EDCS-27600110.summary: This inscription provides the full titulature of Emperor Aurelian, indicating his third consulship and likely dating to his reign (AD 270-275).

EDCS-27600111.translation: To Titus Flavius Valerius Constantius, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-27600111.summary: This is a dedication to the Caesar Constantius, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-27600112.translation: To our Lords Constantius Maximus Augustus and Constantius, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-27600112.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Constantius Maximus and Constantius, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-27600340.translation: Vitulus, a silversmith engraver, aged 24, is buried here, through the care of the guild of silversmiths and the guild of Caesariensians. May the earth rest lightly on you.
EDCS-27600340.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Vitulus, a craftsman, who died at age 24, with his burial arranged by his guilds, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27600344.translation: Tiberius Claudius Flavus of Caesarea, lived 65 years, is buried here. May the earth rest lightly on you. His mother, with her children, made this from his will.
EDCS-27600344.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Flavus, who died at age 65, with arrangements made by his mother and children according to his will, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27600385.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a name, perhaps a dedication or tombstone, from an indeterminate period.

EDCS-27600527.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a name, perhaps a dedication or tombstone, from an indeterminate period.

EDCS-27600564.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription indicating the origin of a product or workshop, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27600598.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription containing a personal name, possibly a tombstone or dedication, from an indeterminate period.

EDCS-27600670.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription containing a personal name, possibly a tombstone or dedication, from an indeterminate period.

EDCS-27600681.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a name, perhaps a dedication or tombstone, from an indeterminate period.

EDCS-27600689.translation: From the workshop of Quintus Sempronius.
EDCS-27600689.summary: This is an owner's mark, likely from the workshop of Quintus Sempronius.

EDCS-27600692.summary: This is an owner's mark, likely from the workshop of Quintus Sempronius.

EDCS-27600779.summary: This inscription records the name Munatius Treptus, possibly as a dedicator or the subject of the monument.

EDCS-27600781.summary: This inscription records the name Munatius Treptus, possibly as a dedicator or the subject of the monument.

EDCS-27600784.summary: This inscription records the genitive name Marcus Novius Justus, likely indicating ownership or dedication.

EDCS-27600785.summary: This inscription records the genitive name Marcus Novius Justus, likely indicating ownership or dedication.

EDCS-27600791.summary: This inscription records the name Gaius Oppius Res, possibly as a dedicator or the subject of the monument.

EDCS-27600792.summary: This inscription records the name Gaius Oppius Res, possibly as a dedicator or the subject of the monument.

EDCS-27600793.summary: This is a personal name, likely part of a longer inscription, belonging to Gaius Oppius Res.

EDCS-27600803.summary: This inscription indicates a product or work originating from the workshop of Pontianus.

EDCS-27600808.summary: This is a personal name, likely part of a longer inscription, belonging to Lucius Sempronius Ha.

EDCS-27600831.summary: This inscription refers to the workshop of Ardax, possibly indicating ownership or origin of an item.

EDCS-27600837.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Gnaeus Ateius Dionysus.

EDCS-27600844.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Lucius Avillius.

EDCS-27800155.translation: Maxiana, aged 11, and VVXI II S.
EDCS-27800155.summary: This inscription likely records the ages of two individuals, Maxiana and another whose name or age is partially preserved.

EDCS-27800156.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Pescennia Saturnina lived 125 years. She made this while healthy; she is buried here.
EDCS-27800156.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pescennia Saturnina, who lived 125 years and made her tomb while healthy.

EDCS-27800157.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Pompeia Sterceia lived 38 years. Iulius Sitifensis made and dedicated this, she is buried here.
EDCS-27800157.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Pompeia Sterceia, who lived 38 years, and was made and dedicated by Iulius Sitifensis.

EDCS-27800158.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Quintia Lollia Aliquas lived 18 years.
EDCS-27800158.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintia Lollia Aliquas, who lived 18 years.

EDCS-27800159.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Saturninus lived 35 years. Luciosa.
EDCS-27800159.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Saturninus, who lived 35 years, with a mention of Luciosa.

EDCS-27800161.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Successa lived 41 years. She is buried here. Matutinus made this for his deserving spouse.
EDCS-27800161.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Matutinus made a tomb for his deserving spouse Successa, who lived 41 years.

EDCS-27800162.translation: Lived 95 years, sacred to the departed spirits. Aulus Titius Amandus and his wife Sabina. You who pass by and read my inscription, say 'Hail Amandus,' and your life will be long. Aulus Titius Victor, son, at his own expense made the eternal home.
EDCS-27800162.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aulus Titius Amandus and his wife Sabina, with an epitaph by their son Aulus Titius Victor, who built their tomb.

EDCS-27800163.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Valerius Victor, son of Gaius, from Assur, lived 70 years. He is buried here. Gaius Valerius Ianuarius, his son, made this.
EDCS-27800163.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Valerius Victor, who lived 70 years, made by his son Gaius Valerius Ianuarius.

EDCS-27800165.translation: To Ulus, the well-deserving; his three brothers Victorinus and Cio, who lived
EDCS-27800165.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Ulus by his three brothers, mentioning Victorinus and Cio and indicating a surviving lifespan.

EDCS-27800166.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Marcus Ulpius Tauliscus lived eighty years; he is buried here.
EDCS-27800166.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Ulpius Tauliscus, who lived to be eighty years old.

EDCS-27800167.translation: Urrentius to his dearest son with S
EDCS-27800167.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Urrentius to his son, with an incomplete phrase following.

EDCS-27800168.translation: To Ania, to his daughter, who lived forty-eight years; she is buried here.
EDCS-27800168.summary: This funerary epitaph records the life and burial of Ania, daughter of M. T. E. Maiiilis, who died at age forty-eight.

EDCS-27800169.translation: Libera made this for her husband, the most innocent P. I. C. Sonvs Valv.
EDCS-27800169.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Libera for her "most innocent" husband, P. I. C. Sonvs Valv.

EDCS-27800170.summary: This inscription is a partial dedication, possibly funerary, to someone named Felisimo.

EDCS-27800172.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription mentioning someone's mother, with an incomplete name or dedication.

EDCS-27800173.translation: To the Divine Shades. Hacmca lived nineteen years.
EDCS-27800173.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Hacmca, who died at the age of nineteen.

EDCS-27800174.translation: F. L. to his wife, who lived seventy years.
EDCS-27800174.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for an unnamed wife of F. L., who lived to be seventy years old.

EDCS-27800177.summary: This is a dedication, possibly funerary, to Ilie and Felix, with an incomplete phrase following.

EDCS-27800178.translation: This is the memorial of the most distinguished and remarkable woman Cypriana, who lived faithfully in peace for 25 years and 4 months, and was received on the 19th of October in the 415th year of the province.
EDCS-27800178.summary: This epitaph records the life and death of Cypriana, a distinguished woman, noting her lifespan and date of reception into peace, likely within the 5th century CE.

EDCS-27800180.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. This memorial, which Flavius Eustasius and Iulia Crescentina his wife made in the name of God and his Christ for their time of deposition, was perfected and dedicated on the Ides of March in the 345th year of the province, [dedicated by] their son Silvanus.
EDCS-27800180.summary: This epitaph, likely from the 4th century CE, records the dedication of a memorial by Flavius Eustasius and Iulia Crescentina for themselves, with their son Silvanus mentioned.

EDCS-27800181.translation: Here lies Gotat ETICVO, who lived 30 years.
EDCS-27800181.summary: This is a simple epitaph recording the name and lifespan of an individual named Gotat ETICVO, likely from late antiquity.

EDCS-27800192.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aurelia Cattina lived 35 years; she lies here. Sacred to the departed spirits. Obulcius Mascel lived 30 years and 3 months.
EDCS-27800192.summary: This epitaph records the lives of two individuals, Aurelia Cattina and Obulcius Mascel, noting their names, lifespans, and placement in the ground.

EDCS-27800194.translation: Numida made this for his Tabita, worthy of her merits; she lies here.
EDCS-27800194.summary: This epitaph records Numida's dedication of a burial place for his Tabita, indicating affection and recognition of her merits.

EDCS-27800195.translation: Primigenius the gatekeeper lies here; he lived 40 years, 3 months, and 9 days.
EDCS-27800195.summary: This epitaph records the name, occupation (gatekeeper), and lifespan of Primigenius.

EDCS-27800196.translation: Spero, son of Migginis the gatekeeper, aged two years, lies here.
EDCS-27800196.summary: This epitaph records the death of a very young child, Spero, son of a gatekeeper, noting his age.

EDCS-27800201.translation: Aurasia, daughter of Gudala, Mazica FOCOLIM, lived 14 years.
EDCS-27800201.summary: This epitaph records the name of Aurasia, her parentage, and her young age at death.

EDCS-27800307.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely an owner's name or a name associated with a property or tomb.

EDCS-27800308.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely indicating the name of a business or workshop.

EDCS-27800323.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop of Matugenus, likely a craftsman or business owner.

EDCS-27800324.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop of Modestus, likely a craftsman or business owner.

EDCS-27800325.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop of Mommo, likely a craftsman or business owner.

EDCS-27800336.summary: This inscription simply records the name Marcus Perennius, possibly a person or a business.

EDCS-27800337.summary: This inscription records the name Perennius Tigranus, possibly a person or a business.

EDCS-27800338.summary: This inscription records the name Quintus Petillius, possibly a person or a business.

EDCS-27800340.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Plotidius Zosimus, possibly a person or a business.

EDCS-28100194.summary: This is a Christian inscription, likely a funerary epitaph or a blessing, invoking Christ.

EDCS-28100197.translation: Flavia, may she live in God.
EDCS-28100197.summary: This is a Christian inscription, likely a funerary epitaph or a blessing for Flavia.

EDCS-28100198.translation: To our Lords, the most pious and fortunate Emperors Valentinian and Valens, born for the good of the state, [---] N L P O.
EDCS-28100198.summary: This is a dedication to Emperors Valentinian and Valens, likely from the 4th century CE, praising them and the Republic.

EDCS-28100199.translation: To our lords Constantinus and Magnentius, always Augusti, born for the good of the state, 2000 paces.
EDCS-28100199.summary: This is a milestone inscription dating to the period of the co-emperors Constantinus and Magnentius (AD 349-350) and records a distance of 2000 paces.

EDCS-28100207.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 2nd time.
EDCS-28100207.summary: This is a dedication for Emperor Septimius Severus, likely on a public monument, mentioning his titles and honors up to his 5th tribunician power and 2nd consulship.

EDCS-28100208.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, and to the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and Geta, from the Lambdesenses, 1000 paces.
EDCS-28100208.summary: This milestone inscription, dating to AD 200-202, records a distance of 1000 paces and is dedicated to Septimius Severus, his son Caracalla, and his other son Geta.

EDCS-28100209.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, pious, happy.
EDCS-28100209.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, likely part of a larger inscription, honoring him with his titles.

EDCS-28100211.translation: To our lord, the Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Maximius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, from Tigavae 3000 paces. To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Flavius Valerius Constantinus and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars, from Tigavae 3000 paces. To our lords Diocletian and Maximian, the Seniors, Augusti, to Constantinus and Maximianus, Emperors, and to Severus and Maximianus, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-28100211.summary: This is a milestone inscription from Tigavae, dating to AD 307-311, recording a distance of 3000 paces and listing numerous emperors and caesars of the Tetrarchy and its aftermath.

EDCS-28100214.translation: To our lords Constantinus, Max... ORCA To our lords
EDCS-28100214.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a milestone, mentioning 'our lords' and possibly Constantius and Maxentius, with an illegible section.

EDCS-28100360.translation: To Antoninus Caesar Augustus, prince of the youth, from Thamalla, 3000 paces.
EDCS-28100360.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance of 3000 paces from Thamalla and is dedicated to Caracalla when he was princeps iuventutis (AD 196-209).

EDCS-28100363.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Flavius Valerius Constantinus and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars, from Auzia Rapidu, 1000 paces.
EDCS-28100363.summary: This milestone inscription, dating to AD 293-305, records a distance of 1000 paces from Auzia Rapidu and is dedicated to the Tetrarchic emperors Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius.

EDCS-28100367.translation: To Flavius Claudius Julianus, most invincible, always Augustus.
EDCS-28100367.summary: This is a dedication to the Emperor Julian, likely on a public monument, honoring him with his imperial titles.

EDCS-28100372.translation: To our lords Constantinus, Constantins, Constantis, 'Magnentius', always victorious Augusti, pious, BR MF, 1000 paces. A.
EDCS-28100372.summary: This milestone inscription, dating to AD 349-350, records a distance of 1000 paces and is dedicated to the co-emperors Constantinus, Constantius, and Magnentius.

EDCS-28200001.translation: Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Publius Septimius Geta nobilissimus Caesar ordered new milestones to be placed at the advance camp, under the supervision of Publius Aelius Peregrinus, procurator of the Emperors, for Cohors I Pannoniorum, 8 miles from Lucius.
EDCS-28200001.summary: This inscription records the placement of new milestones by Emperors Septimius Severus and Geta, supervised by procurator Publius Aelius Peregrinus, for Cohors I Pannoniorum, dating to their joint rule (c. 209-211 AD).

EDCS-28200004.translation: For Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, 11 miles from Lucius.
EDCS-28200004.summary: This inscription records the placement of milestones for Emperor Philip the Arab, dating to his reign (244-249 AD).

EDCS-28200006.translation: For Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Felix Augustus, 8 miles.
EDCS-28200006.summary: This inscription records the placement of milestones for Emperor Claudius Gothicus, dating to his reign (268-270 AD).

EDCS-28200008.translation: Emperor Caesar Severus Antoninus, under the supervision of Munatius Celsus, an outstanding man, procurator of Augustus, 20 miles from Kaputtasaccora.
EDCS-28200008.summary: This inscription records the placement of milestones by Emperor Caracalla, supervised by procurator Munatius Celsus, dating to his sole rule (212-217 AD).

EDCS-28200009.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus, father of the fatherland, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, 19 miles from Kaputtasaccora, under the supervision of Quintus Munatius Celsus, an outstanding man, procurator of our Augustus.
EDCS-28200009.summary: This inscription records the placement of milestones by Emperor Caracalla, supervised by procurator Quintus Munatius Celsus, dating to his fifteenth tribunician power and third consulship (212 AD).

EDCS-28200016.translation: They set up through Publius Sallustius Victor.
EDCS-28200016.summary: This fragmentary inscription records the setting up of something, possibly a monument or milestones, by Publius Sallustius Victor.

EDCS-28200018.translation: Pius Felix Augustus, father of the fatherland, son of Antoninus Caesar, consul.
EDCS-28200018.summary: This fragmentary inscription records titles of an emperor, likely Caracalla, dating to his consulship.

EDCS-28200020.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Sarmaticus, Germanicus, Britannicus, strengthened the province with new ramparts, restored milestones collapsed by age, through...
EDCS-28200020.summary: This inscription records the restoration of milestones and new ramparts by Emperor Commodus, dating to his reign (177-192 AD).

EDCS-28200024.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and Marcus Flavius Constantius and Galerius.
EDCS-28200024.summary: This inscription lists the titles of several emperors, including Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius, likely commemorating a joint project or event during the Tetrarchy (c. 293-305 AD).

EDCS-28200029.translation: To our lord Flavius Claudius Julianus Imperator Pius Felix semper Augustus, the ALA...
EDCS-28200029.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Julian, dating to his reign (360-363 AD), possibly related to a military unit.

EDCS-28200030.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Clodius Pupienus Maximus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul for the second time, proconsul, and to the Emperor Caesar Decimus Caelus Calvinus Balbinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland, and to Marcus Antonius Gordianus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, grandson of the deified Gordians: [a road of] a thousand paces.
EDCS-28200030.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reigns of the emperors Pupienus and Balbinus, with a dedication also mentioning the future emperor Gordian III, recording a stretch of road.

EDCS-28200031.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, father of the fatherland, holder of tribunician power, consul, proconsul, and to Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Geta Augustus: 15 miles [of road].
EDCS-28200031.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance of 15 miles, dedicated to Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla (though Geta is mentioned, the era points to Caracalla's influence).

EDCS-28200032.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, father of the fatherland, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Severus: [a road of] M [paces].
EDCS-28200032.summary: This fragment of a milestone inscription records a road distance and honors Emperor Severus Alexander, mentioning his imperial lineage.

EDCS-28200034.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland: 3 miles [of road] from Portus Magnus.
EDCS-28200034.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance of 3 miles from Portus Magnus, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab.

EDCS-28200035.translation: To Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 19th time, imperator for the third time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, proconsul: 4 miles [of road] from Portus Magnus.
EDCS-28200035.summary: This milestone inscription, likely honoring Emperor Trajan due to the titles, records a distance of 4 miles from Portus Magnus.

EDCS-28200036.translation: To Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, pontifex maximus.
EDCS-28200036.summary: This inscription fragment, possibly from a monument or milestone, honors an emperor with titles related to victories in Britain and Germany.

EDCS-28200039.translation: To our Lord the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland: 5 miles [of road] from Aquae.
EDCS-28200039.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance of 5 miles from Aquae, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab.

EDCS-28200041.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Quintillus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, by the cohort of the Breuci: 1 mile [of road], 4 miles [of road].
EDCS-28200041.summary: This milestone inscription, dedicated by the cohort of the Breuci, records two segments of road distance and honors Emperor Claudius Quintillus.

EDCS-28200042.translation: To the Emperors Carus Pius Felix Augustus and Carinus and Numerian Caesars Felices, by the cohort of the Breuci: 1 mile [of road], 5 miles [of road].
EDCS-28200042.summary: This milestone inscription, dedicated by the cohort of the Breuci, records two segments of road distance and honors the emperors Carus, Carinus, and Numerian.

EDCS-28200043.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul: 7 miles [of road] from the head of the city.
EDCS-28200043.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance of 7 miles from the head of the city, dedicated to Emperor Maximinus Thrax.

EDCS-28200046.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, 3 miles.
EDCS-28200046.summary: This inscription marks a road of 3 miles dedicated to Emperor Maximinus I (reigned 235-238 AD).

EDCS-28200047.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, 3 miles.
EDCS-28200047.summary: This inscription marks a road of 3 miles dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab (reigned 244-249 AD).

EDCS-28200048.translation: To the most noble Caesar Pius G[...?] Philip Augustus, 3 miles from Lucus.
EDCS-28200048.summary: This inscription marks a road of 3 miles dedicated to Caesar Philip (likely Philip I or II, reigned 244-249 AD).

EDCS-28200051.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, chief priest, father of the fatherland, holder of tribunician power, consul, proconsul, 7 miles from Lucus.
EDCS-28200051.summary: This inscription marks a road of 7 miles dedicated to Emperor Severus Alexander (reigned 222-235 AD).

EDCS-28200083.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, Germanicus Maximus, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, emperor for the 2nd time.
EDCS-28200083.summary: This is a titulary inscription for Emperor Caracalla (reigned 198-217 AD), listing his numerous honorific titles and powers.

EDCS-28200086.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Valerius Diocletian, the Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, and to Caesar Emperor Marcus Aurelius Maximian, the Unconquered, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, and to Flavius Valerius Maximian [Caesar]...
EDCS-28200086.summary: This inscription appears to be part of a dedication to multiple emperors, including Diocletian and Maximian (reigned 284-305 AD).

EDCS-28200120.translation: Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus, and Flavius Valerius Constantius, and Galerius Valerius Maximian, most noble Caesars, 1 mile from Tipasa.
EDCS-28200120.summary: This inscription marks a road of 1 mile from Tipasa dedicated to the Tetrarchs: Diocletian, Maximian, Constantius, and Galerius (late 3rd/early 4th century AD).

EDCS-28200122.translation: To our Lords Emperors Flavius Valentinian and Flavius Valens, always Augusti, 2 miles.
EDCS-28200122.summary: This inscription marks a road of 2 miles dedicated to Emperors Valentinian I and Valens (reigned 364-375 AD).

EDCS-28200125.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, consul for the 2nd time, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, son of the divine Antoninus, grandson of the divine Severus, 2 miles from Tipasitani.
EDCS-28200125.summary: This inscription marks a road of 2 miles from Tipasa dedicated to Emperor Elagabalus (reigned 218-222 AD), listing his descent and titles.

EDCS-28200126.translation: To our Lords Emperors, Caesars, Trebonianus Gallus for the second time and Afinius Volusianus, Augusti, consul, 3 miles from Tipasitani.
EDCS-28200126.summary: This inscription marks a road of 3 miles from Tipasa dedicated to the short-lived emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus (reigned 251-253 AD).

EDCS-28200127.translation: To our most provident Lords Constantinus and Constantius, always Augusti, 5,000 paces. To our Lord Julian, the Emperor, always Augusti. To our Lords Valentinian and Valens, Emperors, always Augusti, perpetual.
EDCS-28200127.summary: This inscription records milestones along a road, dedicated to multiple Roman emperors from the Constantinian and Valentinianic periods.

EDCS-28200128.translation: To the Emperors Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus, pious, fortunate, twice Consul, Pontifex Maximus, father of the fatherland, Proconsul, and to Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, pious, fortunate, twice Consul, 2,000 paces.
EDCS-28200128.summary: This inscription records milestones indicating a road of 2,000 paces, dedicated to the emperors Macrinus and his son Diadumenianus, dated to their joint consulship (AD 218).

EDCS-28200129.translation: To our Lords, Emperors Caesar Trebonianus Gallus, twice Consul, and Afinius Gallus Vindex Volusianus, Augusti, 2,000 paces.
EDCS-28200129.summary: This inscription records milestones for a road of 2,000 paces, dedicated to the short-lived emperors Trebonianus Gallus and Volusianus, dated to AD 252.

EDCS-28200130.translation: To our Lord, Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus, unconquered, pious, fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power, Consul, father of the fatherland, Proconsul, 2,000 paces.
EDCS-28200130.summary: This inscription records milestones for a road of 2,000 paces, dedicated to the emperor Aurelian, likely during his reign (AD 270-275).

EDCS-28200131.translation: To the Emperors Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, pious, fortunate, unconquered, Augustus, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerian Maximian, pious, fortunate, unconquered, Augustus, and to the most noble Caesars Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximian, 2,000 paces from Tipasa.
EDCS-28200131.summary: This inscription records milestones for a road of 2,000 paces from Tipasa, dedicated to the Tetrarchic emperors Diocletian and Maximian, and their Caesars Constantius and Galerius Maximian, dated between AD 293 and 305.

EDCS-28200169.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, father of the fatherland, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the third time, Imperator for the fourth time, Consul twice, Proconsul, restored the milestones.
EDCS-28200169.summary: This inscription records the restoration of milestones by Emperor Septimius Severus, dated to AD 193-211 based on his titles.

EDCS-28200170.translation: To our Lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus the Younger, most noble Caesar, 4,000 paces.
EDCS-28200170.summary: This inscription records milestones indicating a road of 4,000 paces, dedicated to the Caesar Constantine Iunior, likely in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-28300493.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, son of the divine Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the divine Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the eighth time, Consul three times, Proconsul.
EDCS-28300493.summary: This inscription lists the full titles of Emperor Hadrian, likely displayed on a public monument, dated to around AD 125-128 based on his tribunician power and consulships.

EDCS-30100148.translation: The Germanic tribes of the Bavarii, whose entire host was routed, with their kings Taganis, Masmulus, and Fahem slain, and Afra, Fasa, and Vivisicus captured, and the royal spoils collected, Sulpicius Sacratus paid his vows to the immortal gods and the Genius of the place.
EDCS-30100148.summary: This inscription records a military victory over the Bavarii tribe by Sulpicius Sacratus, who dedicates spoils and vows to the gods and local genius, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-30501156.translation: He lived for 68 years. He lies buried here.
EDCS-30501156.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the age and burial of an unnamed individual.

EDCS-31300215.translation: Marco Domizio Rufo, master of liberal studies, a good man, lived 75 years.
EDCS-31300215.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commemorating a man named Marco Domizio Rufo, who was a teacher and lived to be 75 years old.

EDCS-31300239.translation: You, now that the rebellious Frumentius has been subdued by virtue, look back and restore the baths covered in reeds.
EDCS-31300239.summary: This is a programmatic or dedicatory text, possibly related to the restoration of baths, referencing a military victory over someone named Frumentius.

EDCS-31500017.translation: Here rests Fortunata, a little girl of good memory, laid to rest in peace on the seventh day before the Kalends of July.
EDCS-31500017.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl named Fortunata, with her date of death recorded.

EDCS-33100002.translation: Sextus Pompeius Saturninus of Ticinum paid a votive offering to Tellus (the Earth goddess) with his own money and four silver palms.
EDCS-33100002.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to the goddess Tellus by Sextus Pompeius Saturninus, funded by himself and including silver palms.

EDCS-33500109.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the name of a woman and her father, possibly on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-33500111.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording a personal name and patronymic, possibly from a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-33500112.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Rasinius Pisanus, possibly as an owner of property or a tomb.

EDCS-33500118.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Rasinius Pisanus, possibly as an owner of property or a tomb.

EDCS-33500120.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Rasinius Pisanus, possibly as an owner of property or a tomb.

EDCS-33500121.summary: This inscription records the name of a workshop, indicating it belongs to or is named after Sabinus.

EDCS-33500122.summary: This is a personal name, Lucius Rasinius Pisanus, likely a dedication or property owner.

EDCS-33500123.summary: This inscription records the name Princeps, son of Lucius Gellius, possibly a dedication or name on a monument.

EDCS-33500126.summary: This inscription records the name Primigenius, son of Rasinius, likely a dedication or property owner.

EDCS-33500127.summary: This inscription records the name Primigenius, son of Lucius Titus, likely a dedication or property owner.

EDCS-33500129.summary: This inscription records the personal name Lucius Rasinius Draucus, likely a dedication or property owner.

EDCS-33600084.summary: This is a personal name, Marcus Valerius, likely a dedication or property owner.

EDCS-33600087.summary: This is a personal name, Caius Volusenus, likely a dedication or property owner.

EDCS-33600089.summary: This inscription records a person as the son of Lucius Valerius Titus, likely a dedication or property owner.

EDCS-33600094.summary: This inscription records that Lucius Umbricius made this object or monument.

EDCS-33600095.summary: This inscription records the name Philogenes, son of Vilius, likely a dedication or property owner.

EDCS-33600100.summary: This inscription is a genitive personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Marcus Valerius Volusus.

EDCS-33600101.summary: This inscription is a nominative personal name, likely identifying Marcus Valerius Volusus.

EDCS-33600239.summary: This inscription is a genitive personal name, likely indicating ownership or a dedication by Marcus Valerius Salvius.

EDCS-33600423.summary: This inscription records a personal name, Lucius Titius, possibly with emphasis through repetition or error.

EDCS-33600424.summary: This inscription records the personal name Caius Titius Nepos.

EDCS-33600425.summary: This inscription records the personal name Caius Tetius, with 'lax' possibly being a cognomen or an incomplete word.

EDCS-33600429.translation: Of Surisus, son of Lucius Nonius
EDCS-33600429.summary: This inscription is a genitive personal name, indicating ownership or a dedication by Surisus, son of Lucius Nonius.

EDCS-33600432.summary: This inscription records the personal name Quintus Sulpicius.

EDCS-33600433.summary: This inscription records the personal name Sertorius Proculus.

EDCS-33600437.summary: This inscription indicates the 'Workshop of Severus', likely referring to a business or artisanal establishment.

EDCS-33700114.summary: This inscription indicates the ownership of a workshop by Luccius.

EDCS-33700118.summary: This inscription names Sextus Iulius Aprilis, likely a person's full name.

EDCS-33700119.summary: This inscription indicates the ownership of a workshop by Iulius Pudentis.

EDCS-33700130.summary: This inscription identifies Hilarus and his lineage, noting he is the grandson of Titus.

EDCS-33700479.summary: This inscription indicates the ownership of a workshop by Logirnus.

EDCS-33700481.summary: This inscription names Caius Clodius Sabinus, likely a person's full name.

EDCS-33700482.summary: This inscription records a payment or allocation related to Constantine, consisting of 6 ounces and 36 solidi, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-33700507.summary: This inscription names Publius Cornelius Priscus, likely a person's full name.

EDCS-33700509.summary: This inscription names Publius Cornelius, likely a person's full name.

EDCS-33700511.summary: This inscription names Publius Cornelius, likely a person's full name.

EDCS-33700515.summary: This is a name, likely an owner or a person honored, possibly from a tombstone or other personal monument.

EDCS-33700517.summary: This is a full name, likely an owner or a person honored, possibly from a tombstone or other personal monument.

EDCS-33700518.summary: This is a full name, likely an owner or a person honored, possibly from a tombstone or other personal monument.

EDCS-33700519.summary: This is a full name, likely an owner or a person honored, possibly from a tombstone or other personal monument.

EDCS-33700525.summary: This inscription records the name of Chryseros, son of Sextus Vibius, likely a freedman or a person of Greek origin, possibly on a tombstone.

EDCS-33700528.summary: This inscription records the name of Caius Caelius Crispus, likely indicating ownership or a dedication, possibly on a funerary monument or property marker.

EDCS-33700534.translation: May you live in the god
EDCS-33700534.summary: This is a religious formula, possibly a wish for the deceased or a statement of belief, found on a funerary or devotional object.

EDCS-33700583.summary: This inscription records the name of Titus Flavius Aurelius Aristobolus, likely indicating ownership or a dedication, possibly on a funerary monument or property marker.

EDCS-34300773.translation: Our lord Julian, ever victorious and triumphant Augustus
EDCS-34300773.summary: This is a formal imperial title and acclamation for Emperor Julian, likely inscribed on a public monument or statue during his reign in the 4th century AD.

EDCS-34300774.translation: Of our lord Julian, ever victorious Augustus
EDCS-34300774.summary: This inscription refers to Emperor Julian, likely part of a longer dedication or possessive phrase related to a building or object, dating to his reign in the 4th century AD.

EDCS-34500129.translation: Doliary ware from the estates of our emperor Augustus, from Caius Cominius Sabinianus.
EDCS-34500129.summary: This is a private document concerning the production and ownership of pottery from imperial estates, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-35200304.translation: Prohibiting evil, the earth to be covered by war and to defile your sacred body and faithful for twenty-four years and months and days, you have been carried well by the earth.
EDCS-35200304.summary: This is a fragment of a text, possibly a curse or a lament, referencing war, defilement, and a period of 24 years, its origin and date are uncertain.

EDCS-39200520.translation: Hope, the good soul. To the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-39200520.summary: This is a funerary inscription invoking the 'good soul' and dedicating it to the Dis Manibus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39200558.translation: To the Gods of the Underworld. Pithousa the Hellenic woman, aged 40. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-39200558.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a Greek woman named Pithousa, dedicating herself to the chthonic gods and requesting light earth, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39200564.translation: I, Publius Proclus, a youth, lie here, having completed two decades of years, but I did not complete the sweet light of life, but Fate led me to Hades and took away my life swiftly. And she gave to my father without pity bitter laments and tears. Since you are old, O lovely Nysa, grant to me, the unfortunate, an eternal portion of youth.
EDCS-39200564.summary: This is a funerary epigram for a young man named Proclus, lamenting his premature death and asking the Muse Nysa to grant him eternal youth in the afterlife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39200566.translation: Carminius of Thessalonica. To the Chthonic Gods. Fate granted me a short time, and I did not even complete this short time, but died, having delighted many with my sweet songs. Weep for Carminius, dying so young.
EDCS-39200566.summary: This is a funerary epigram for a musician named Carminius of Thessalonica, who died young after a short life filled with song, dedicated to the chthonic gods, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-39200571.translation: Zosimus, a youth, LOS is the name, Euripides for his father. I lie here, having come to life for a short time. Alexander, aged 22.
EDCS-39200571.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young man named Zosimus (also called Alexander), whose father was named Euripides, and possibly adopted the name Zosimus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-41300002.translation: A beneficiary of the consular governor, a candidate from his own municipality.
EDCS-41300002.summary: This inscription refers to a military or administrative official serving as a beneficiarius under a consular governor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-41500042.translation: Donatus, master of the guild, life.
EDCS-41500042.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly commemorating a guild master named Donatus, the meaning is incomplete, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-41500055.translation: The memory of our father Lucius, bishop, lasted in the episcopate for twenty-two years and five months, in peace.
EDCS-41500055.summary: This is an epitaph for Lucius, a bishop who served for 22 years and 5 months, likely from the early Christian period.

EDCS-42600192.translation: Here is the memorial of the saints Vincent, Felix, Constantia, and Victoria, placed by Bishop Saint Cresciturus on the third day before the Kalends of April. Amen.
EDCS-42600192.summary: This inscription records the placement of a memorial for four saints by Bishop Cresciturus on March 30th.

EDCS-43400001.translation: To the departed spirits, Aelius Victor lived 25 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-43400001.summary: This is a tombstone for Aelius Victor, recording his age and burial.

EDCS-43400002.translation: To the departed spirits, Publius Cornelius Honoratus, son of Cai, lived 10 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-43400002.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Cornelius Honoratus, recording his age and burial.

EDCS-43400003.translation: To the departed spirits, Quintus Pempulius Caecilianus of Ariminum lived 45 years; he is buried here.
EDCS-43400003.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Pempulius Caecilianus, recording his age and burial.

EDCS-43400005.summary: This is a dedication by Aurelius to Ianuaria.

EDCS-43400006.translation: To the departed spirits, Claudia Spes lived 16 years, 2 months, and 8 days; may the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-43400006.summary: This is a tombstone for Claudia Spes, recording her age and a common funerary wish.

EDCS-43400007.translation: To the departed spirits, Marcus Iulius Sa[---]
EDCS-43400007.summary: This inscription begins a dedication to the departed spirits for Marcus Iulius Sa[---].

EDCS-43400008.translation: Mimosa placed this table for her most beloved son, Dato.
EDCS-43400008.summary: This inscription records Mimosa setting up a funerary table for her son Dato.

EDCS-43400009.translation: Severianus made this table for Tutita, his daughter.
EDCS-43400009.summary: This inscription records Severianus making a funerary table for his daughter Tutita.

EDCS-43400010.translation: Soricas made this table in memory of Anna.
EDCS-43400010.summary: This inscription records Soricas making a funerary table in memory of Anna.

EDCS-43400011.translation: Martialis lived 95 years and 3 months. Voconius Caudiosus, also called Cai, [erected this] for the most innocent.
EDCS-43400011.summary: This epitaph records the lifespan of Martialis and was set up by Voconius Caudiosus.

EDCS-43400012.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Aelius Vibius lived 45 years, 2 months, and 5 days. Aelia Saturnina lived 20 years and [some] months.
EDCS-43400012.summary: This epitaph records the lifespans of Aelius Vibius and Aelia Saturnina.

EDCS-43400017.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Valerio Tonanti, an Augustan cavalryman of the Thracian ala, who lived [some years]. Tertiolus and Pius set this up for their most devoted brother.
EDCS-43400017.summary: This epitaph is dedicated to Valerio Tonanti by his brothers Tertiolus and Pius.

EDCS-43400019.translation: Acrisius Crescens, 2 months and 1 day old.
EDCS-43400019.summary: This epitaph records the brief lifespan of Acrisius Crescens.

EDCS-43400034.translation: For our lord Claudius Constantinus the Younger, most noble Caesar, 3 miles.
EDCS-43400034.summary: This inscription likely marks a distance of 3 miles for the Emperor Claudius Constantinus the Younger.

EDCS-43400357.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedicatory inscription, possibly on a tomb or object.

EDCS-43400362.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedicatory inscription, possibly on a tomb or object.

EDCS-43400402.translation: Belonging to Sartiva, wife of Lucius Tettius.
EDCS-43400402.summary: This inscription likely records ownership or dedicates something to Sartiva, wife of Lucius Tettius.

EDCS-43400410.translation: Belonging to Lucius Titius, son of Lucius Titius.
EDCS-43400410.summary: This inscription likely records ownership or dedicates something to Lucius Titius, son of Lucius Titius.

EDCS-43400412.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedicatory inscription, possibly on a tomb or object.

EDCS-43400414.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely referring to a person named Marcus Valerius Volusus.

EDCS-43400839.summary: This is a very brief inscription commemorating a person named Caecilianus.

EDCS-43500006.translation: To the Divine Manes. Cornelia made this for Cornelius Eutrapelus, her pious husband, a worthy husband. He is buried here.
EDCS-43500006.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Cornelia to her husband Cornelius Eutrapelus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-43500034.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. In memory of Considius Secundus, who preceded us in the Lord's peace. He lived for more or less 25 years, and departed on the 5th day before the Ides of June in the 345th year of the province.
EDCS-43500034.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Considius Secundus, dating to the provincial year 345 (around 291-312 AD) and mentioning Christian terminology ('in pace dominica').

EDCS-43500035.translation: Caecilius Fu fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-43500035.summary: This is an inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Caecilius Fu.

EDCS-43500047.translation: And his sons, Iunius Amaius, soldiers of the CAI [= Cohors Alaria Italica?], Theseus the freedman, a banker, Lucius I A Victoricus the freedman, a notary, Bo [---] Hyacinthus the tutor.
EDCS-43500047.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, including soldiers, freedmen working as a banker and notary, and a tutor, likely associated with a funerary monument or a collegium.

EDCS-43500048.translation: Ampalus, son of Camalus, an *eques* of the same cavalry regiment, of the troop of Placidus, arranged for this to be made by his heir according to his will. Titus T P. May the earth be light on you.
EDCS-43500048.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by the heir of Ampalus, an *eques* (cavalryman) in a Roman auxiliary unit, following Ampalus's will.

EDCS-43500049.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Gaius Sentius Saturninus, a deserving veteran. He lived 46 years. May the earth be light on you.
EDCS-43500049.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Sentius Saturninus, a deserving veteran, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-43500054.translation: [...]nia Ianuaria lived 65 years. Who, after her children and husband, Pomaria, having died, [...] I lived 10 years PVAITIIVI Ianuaria lived 9 years, and one month Pai ia.
EDCS-43500054.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph for at least two individuals named Ianuaria, one of whom lived 65 years and another who lived 9 years and a month, possibly indicating a mother and daughter.

EDCS-43500055.translation: To Quintus Geminius Severinus, son of Quintus, of the voting tribe Quirina, curator and administrator of public affairs, a good man, a lover of the state and its citizens, his friends, deservedly.
EDCS-43500055.summary: This is an honorary inscription or epitaph for Quintus Geminius Severinus, who held public office, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-43600096.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Valerius for Germana and Tempula, likely a dedication of a tomb or monument.

EDCS-43700639.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Minucia Victoria lived 70 years.
EDCS-43700639.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Minucia Victoria after 70 years of life.

EDCS-43700640.translation: Pabirius Peregrinus made and built this, dedicating his vow willingly at his own expense.
EDCS-43700640.summary: This inscription records Pabirius Peregrinus dedicating a structure or offering, which he financed and built himself.

EDCS-43700641.translation: Caius Iulius Fortunatus lived 70 years and 10 months; he died on the 14th day before the Kalends of June.
EDCS-43700641.summary: This is a funerary epitaph stating the lifespan and date of death of Caius Iulius Fortunatus.

EDCS-43700684.translation: Fidelis Aurelia Matrona lived more or less 91 years; she departed in peace of our Lord on the 12th day before the Kalends of September in the 505th year of the province.
EDCS-43700684.summary: This Christian funerary inscription records the lifespan and date of death of Aurelia Matrona, likely dating to the Roman province's era.

EDCS-43700714.translation: Adrastus the bishop, in the year of the building and foundation, restored [this] of his father Pria.
EDCS-43700714.summary: This inscription records bishop Adrastus restoring a building, likely a church, in a specific year related to its construction and a provincial dating system.

EDCS-44100005.translation: To the Omnipotent Gods. Verus, procurator of our Augusti.
EDCS-44100005.summary: This is a dedication to the gods by Verus, an imperial procurator, likely on behalf of the emperors.

EDCS-44100201.translation: To our lord the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Traianus Decius Augustus.
EDCS-44100201.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperor Decius, likely part of a public monument or dedication.

EDCS-44100204.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Terentius Namfamo Etepica dedicated this to Ultor, her husband, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-44100204.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Terentius Namfamo dedicates a memorial to her husband Ultor, who lived 60 years.

EDCS-44100205.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Ponivus Suruc, pious, lived 65 years.
EDCS-44100205.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of the pious Ponivus Suruc after 65 years of life.

EDCS-44100206.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Celia Cen lived 5 years. Reoudae lived 5 years.
EDCS-44100206.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Celia Cen and Reoudae, likely young children, providing their names and ages at death.

EDCS-44200057.translation: To our Lords Emperors Constantine the Great and Constantine the Younger and to Julius Constantius, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-44200057.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Constantine the Great, Constantine the Younger, and Julius Constantius, likely dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-44200341.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the divine Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, and to Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camp, senate, and fatherland, son of the divine Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, grandson of the divine Antoninus, great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Maximus, chief priest, with the tribunician power for the 18th time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul. The republic of the Thamallulensians dedicated to Antoninus, keeper of the milestones, for 4 Roman miles.
EDCS-44200341.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Caracalla (Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus) by the community of Thamugadi, dating to approximately 212-213 CE based on tribunician and consulship counts.

EDCS-44200343.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For the memory of Julia Aperta, most pious mother. Caesonius Dec and Flavia (his) daughter placed (this) for remembrance, with Postumius Sossius as father.
EDCS-44200343.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julia Aperta, erected by her son Caesonius Dec and granddaughter Flavia, mentioning her piety and a father figure, Postumius Sossius.

EDCS-44200344.translation: To the memory of Sossianus Sossianus, sweet brother, who lived 25 years, 11 months, and 22 days. Caesonius Dec and Sossia Flavia, (his) sister, placed (this) for his merits.
EDCS-44200344.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sossianus Sossianus, erected by his brother Caesonius Dec and sister Sossia Flavia, providing his name, relationship, and exact lifespan.

EDCS-44200345.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For the memory of Aurelius Biracionis, a knight. He served for 13 years, lived 35 years. Aurelius Ianuarius dedicated (this) to his brother.
EDCS-44200345.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Biracionis, a knight, recording his military service and lifespan, dedicated by his brother Aurelius Ianuarius.

EDCS-44400163.translation: To Emperor Caesar, the unconquered, pious, fortunate Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, to the most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, our lord. For the 4th time.
EDCS-44400163.summary: This is a dedication to an emperor, likely Maximinus Thrax or Decius, referencing his titles and possibly a fourth iteration of something (e.g., acclamation or consulship).

EDCS-44400343.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius, the unconquered, pious, fortunate Augustus. For 4 Roman miles.
EDCS-44400343.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Claudius Gothicus, marking a distance of 4 Roman miles.

EDCS-44400344.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Cerialis. (lived) years.
EDCS-44400344.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph for Cerialis, only providing his name and indicating a lost lifespan.

EDCS-44900022.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and all the immortal gods. Gratefully acknowledging the defeat of the IILEMI people, I, Aurelius Litua, a most accomplished man, governor of the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, freely set up (this offering) because I returned safe and sound with all the soldiers of our Lords Diocletian and Maximian Augustus.
EDCS-44900022.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Litua, governor of Mauretania Caesariensis, thanking the gods for his safe return with his troops after a campaign against the IILEMI people, likely dating to the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-44900023.translation: Agidda to herself and to Marcus Julius Faustus, her most dutiful mother-in-law, according to the will.
EDCS-44900023.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Agidda to herself and her mother-in-law, Marcus Julius Faustus, as stipulated in a will.

EDCS-44900024.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Iulia Sissoi lived 30 years.
EDCS-44900024.summary: A funerary inscription recording the age at death of Iulia Sissoi.

EDCS-44900118.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Quintus Julius Seranus lived 3 years, 6 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-44900118.summary: A funerary inscription recording the age at death of Quintus Julius Seranus, a child.

EDCS-44900132.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Galia Donata, a simple girl, who lived 4 years.
EDCS-44900132.summary: A funerary inscription recording the age at death of Galia Donata, a young girl.

EDCS-44900134.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Onesimus, son of N Ticenus, his mother, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-44900134.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated by Onesimus to his mother, N. Ticenus, who lived to 60.

EDCS-44900135.summary: A brief memorial inscription for Caecilianus.

EDCS-44900136.translation: In memory of Quodvultdeus, who lived 54 years; he was received into peace.
EDCS-44900136.summary: A memorial inscription for Quodvultdeus, noting his age and peaceful reception, likely Christian.

EDCS-44900137.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Maria Prima, daughter of Augustalis, who lived 30 years. Vettius Gracilis, her husband, made this.
EDCS-44900137.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Vettius Gracilis for his wife, Maria Prima, who died at 30.

EDCS-45200152.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Sallustia Concordia lived 57 years, 7 months. Caelia Caeliana lived 20 years, 5 months. Caius Sallustius Commodus Felicianus lived 34 years, 4 months, and 8 days. He is buried here.
EDCS-45200152.summary: A tombstone recording the ages and lifespans of three individuals, Sallustia Concordia, Caelia Caeliana, and Caius Sallustius Commodus Felicianus, indicating they are buried together.

EDCS-45200153.translation: For the Fortui, the 19th day before the Kalends of January.
EDCS-45200153.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly related to a dedication or funerary marker for a group called 'Fortui' on a specific date.

EDCS-45300017.translation: To the good memory of Julius Castus, most loving and most innocent child. Repentius and Quintilia (made this) for their son, and Faventius, his grandfather.
EDCS-45300017.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a child named Julius Castus, set up by his parents and grandfather.

EDCS-45300114.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name on a tomb or object, possibly belonging to Caecilius Capito.

EDCS-45300115.summary: This inscription marks a place belonging to Beonius Pardalus, possibly a grave or property.

EDCS-45500012.translation: He lived 53 years, 3 months, 13 days. His mother Apollonia, his wife Sinama, his father-in-law Curia, his grandfather Iles, and his genuine parents brought him up with their own strength. He preferred to be a leader rather than follow the ways of parents; he remembered his friends and gave to his own; he brought this with him in death. He lived. He lived with reputation. Mensa Apollonia.
EDCS-45500012.summary: This is a lengthy funerary epitaph detailing the life and virtues of the deceased, set up by his mother Apollonia, wife Sinama, and other relatives.

EDCS-45500018.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Occius Castus, while still alive, made this monument to leave a record of his life for himself and for his descendants, because love is similar to us, who has suffered the same things can easily have mercy.
EDCS-45500018.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Titus Occius Castus for himself and his descendants, expressing the sentiment of shared human experience and empathy.

EDCS-45500022.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Diogenes, son of Pharnaces, made this while alive, and prepared an end for all my own. I lived 81 years. And you who read this, do not be surprised, death awaits everyone. May the earth be light upon him.
EDCS-45500022.summary: This is a funerary epitaph created by Diogenes, son of Pharnaces, for himself and his family, reflecting on mortality and setting an example for others.

EDCS-45600051.translation: To Publius Emeritus, Aconia Lucilla (dedicates this) to her most innocent father.
EDCS-45600051.summary: This is a funerary dedication from Aconia Lucilla to her father, Publius Emeritus.

EDCS-45600128.translation: To Julia Tertulla, his dearest wife. Julius Victor, her husband. She lived 70 years.
EDCS-45600128.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Julia Tertulla, set up by her husband Julius Victor, also noting her age at death.

EDCS-45600147.summary: This is a brief inscription, likely identifying ownership or a tomb, belonging to Potamiolus, son of Paulus.

EDCS-45600148.summary: This inscription identifies an individual named Flaionatus who held the position of curator.

EDCS-45600149.translation: Of Flavius the Freedman, his own property.
EDCS-45600149.summary: This inscription likely refers to a property belonging to a freedman named Flavius.

EDCS-45900008.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Julius Leamdianus, his dearest son, fulfilled his vow. He lived 21 years. To the Spirits of the Departed.
EDCS-45900008.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Marcus Julius Leamdianus, son of the author, who lived 21 years.

EDCS-45900009.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Urbanus Mastilius, formerly prefect of the cohort, erected this monument for himself and his family during his lifetime.
EDCS-45900009.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelius Urbanus Mastilius, a former cohort prefect, for himself and his family.

EDCS-45900013.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Claudius Licco, a knight of the Thracian cavalry, who lived 40 years, and Aelia Minna, who lived 80 years. Claudius Licco and Claudia Calpurniana, their children, erected this for their well-deserving parents.
EDCS-45900013.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudius Licco and Aelia Minna, erected by their children, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-45900027.translation: To our Lords Emperors Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Semper Augustus, and Flavius Claudius Constantinus, and Flavius Julius Constantinus.
EDCS-45900027.summary: This inscription honours the emperors Constantine I, Constantine II, and Constantius II, likely erected during their joint reign.

EDCS-45900029.translation: To our Lord Emperor Flavius Claudius Julianus Pius Felix Semper Augustus, a thousand paces for the good of the republic born of the divine Emperors Iovian and Valentinian.
EDCS-45900029.summary: This inscription commemorates a road segment ('a thousand paces') dedicated to Emperor Julian by the republic, possibly relating to the time of Emperors Jovian and Valentinian.

EDCS-45900030.translation: A thousand paces to Emperor Flavius Claudius Julianus Pius Felix Semper Augustus, for the good of the republic.
EDCS-45900030.summary: This inscription records a thousand paces dedicated to Emperor Julian for the benefit of the republic.

EDCS-45900038.translation: Cattia Ianuaria to her most excellent and deserving wife, Mauretania II, 120.
EDCS-45900038.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Cattia Ianuaria to her wife, likely in Roman Mauretania, with a numerical reference possibly indicating a date or distance.

EDCS-45900044.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Gessia Avita, daughter of Gessius Pudentis, patroness, well-deserving. Campanus and Scaeva, her freedmen, erected this. She lived 45 years.
EDCS-45900044.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to Gessia Avita by her freedmen, Campanus and Scaeva.

EDCS-45900045.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. C. Rogatus, son, lived 52 years.
EDCS-45900045.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of C. Rogatus, son, who lived 52 years.

EDCS-46100488.translation: To Antonio Crescenti, my most beloved husband, Fabricia Donata [dedicated this].
EDCS-46100488.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from an unknown location, dedicated by Fabricia Donata to her husband Antonio Crescenti.

EDCS-46100491.translation: Publius Livius Phileros, freedman of Publius Livius Eusextus, an oil seller, lies buried here; he lived 48 years. Valeria made this tombstone for her deserving husband.
EDCS-46100491.summary: This funerary epitaph records the burial of Publius Livius Phileros, an oil seller, and was dedicated by his wife Valeria, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46100492.translation: Lucius Aemilius Cerdippus lies buried here.
EDCS-46100492.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph recording the burial of Lucius Aemilius Cerdippus.

EDCS-46100493.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Papirius Soricio lived 18 years. Papirius Extricatus made this for his well-deserving brother. He lies buried here.
EDCS-46100493.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Papirius Soricio at age 18 and was dedicated by his brother Papirius Extricatus.

EDCS-46100494.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Julia Laevita and Aelius Barlaha, parents. Rosa, their daughter, lived 80 years.
EDCS-46100494.summary: This funerary epitaph is dedicated to parents Iulia Laevita and Aelius Barlaha by their daughter Rosa, who also lived to 80 years old.

EDCS-46100505.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Aelia Germana, who lived 70 years and died on the 18th day before the Kalends of September, [this monument] was set up by her children Iulius Crescens and Massuca in the 323rd year of the province.
EDCS-46100505.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Aelia Germana and was erected by her children Iulius Crescens and Massuca in the provincial year 323 (likely 323 CE or AD).

EDCS-46300003.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Cassia Inquisita lived 18 years. Caius Domitius Iulianus [dedicated this] to his most longed-for wife.
EDCS-46300003.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Cassia Inquisita at age 18 and was dedicated by her husband Caius Domitius Iulianus.

EDCS-46300004.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, sacred. Cominius Crescens lived 6 years, 9 months, and 28 days. Hilarus, your father, may the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-46300004.summary: This funerary epitaph records the short life of Cominius Crescens and includes a dedication from his father, Hilarus.

EDCS-46300006.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcia Namentia lived 30 years; she lies buried here.
EDCS-46300006.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph recording the burial of Marcia Namentia at age 30.

EDCS-46300007.translation: To Naevia Benedicta. Eutychus, her husband, and Heliodorus, her son, took care to have this made for their well-deserving wife and mother. She lived 45 years.
EDCS-46300007.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Naevia Benedicta at age 45 and was commissioned by her husband Eutychus and son Heliodorus.

EDCS-46300008.summary: This inscription indicates the origin of a product from the workshop of Sempronius.

EDCS-46300009.translation: Aufidia, daughter of Aufidius, Caius's daughter.
EDCS-46300009.summary: This is a fragmentary Roman naming convention, likely part of a larger inscription referring to Aufidia.

EDCS-46300017.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Bombilius Felix lived 66 years for Aemilia Maxima, his wife, who lived 46 years.
EDCS-46300017.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Bombilius Felix and his wife Aemilia Maxima, recording their ages at death.

EDCS-46300049.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Sammalus Firmianus lived 56 years for his son; Sammia Aemerita, his mother.
EDCS-46300049.summary: This funerary epitaph records the age of Sammalus Firmianus and is dedicated by his mother, Sammia Aemerita.

EDCS-46300050.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Muntanus lived 50 years.
EDCS-46300050.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Muntanus at death.

EDCS-46300051.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Petilius Victor lived 40 years.
EDCS-46300051.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Lucius Petilius Victor at death.

EDCS-46300053.translation: Lived 75 years. Laelius Peregrinus, son of 'Ira' of his mother.
EDCS-46300053.summary: This epitaph records the age of Laelius Peregrinus and mentions his mother, whose name may have been 'Ira' or indicates her temperament.

EDCS-46300060.translation: Flavia Donata lived 64 years. Her sons and grandsons made this.
EDCS-46300060.summary: This funerary epitaph records the age of Flavia Donata and states it was erected by her descendants.

EDCS-46300061.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Iulius Citinus lived 30 years. Caecilia Nina, his mother, made this.
EDCS-46300061.summary: This funerary epitaph records the age of Iulius Citinus and was made by his mother, Caecilia Nina.

EDCS-46300062.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Lucius Crescens lived 85 years.
EDCS-46300062.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the age of Lucius Crescens at death.

EDCS-46300063.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Clodius Victor lived 65 years.
EDCS-46300063.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Quintus Clodius Victor, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300064.translation: Dionysius erected (this) for himself and Seia the virgin.
EDCS-46300064.summary: This funerary inscription records Dionysius dedicating a monument to himself and Seia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300065.translation: Esuvius Aenulus lived 5 years. Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. I, Mamilia, lived 28 years. Gavilia, daughter, is buried here. Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Esuvius EPIRE VA is buried here.
EDCS-46300065.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument commemorating multiple individuals, including children and adults, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300066.translation: Claudius Caelius lived 17 years. The matron and Donatila C. N. C. Victorina made (this). Mevius E IE made (this).
EDCS-46300066.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Claudius Caelius and mentions the makers of the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300068.translation: Livonnius Rogatus lived 42 years, 5 months, 15 days, and 5 hours.
EDCS-46300068.summary: This funerary inscription precisely records the lifespan of Livonnius Rogatus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300071.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Secunda lived 35 years. Acibas, husband, is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-46300071.summary: This funerary inscription records the lifespan of Secunda and her relationship to Acibas, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300072.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the underworld. Caius Caecilius Saturninus made (this) for his well-deserving son, who lived 5 years, 8 months, and 6 days.
EDCS-46300072.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Caius Caecilius Saturninus dedicated a monument to his son, providing the son's precise age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300073.translation: To Argentarius, who lived 20 years, buried here. His colleagues made the monument.
EDCS-46300073.summary: This funerary inscription records the age of Argentarius and states that his colleagues erected the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300074.translation: To the memory of Marcus Licinius Honoratus, Roman knight. Eternal home. He lived 62 years, 10 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-46300074.summary: This funerary inscription records the age and status (Roman knight) of Marcus Licinius Honoratus, and the concept of an 'eternal home,' likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300075.translation: Marcus Annius Quartus is buried here. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-46300075.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the burial of Marcus Annius Quartus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46300076.summary: This is a funerary inscription identifying the burial place of Gaius Annius R.

EDCS-46300179.translation: Sulpicius Secundus lived for 60 years. Sulpicius Carus lived for 12 years. Sulpicia Basilla lived for 11 years. Clodia Donatula. Sulpicius Lucidus lived for 17 years. Sulpicius Ianuarius [restored] parents.
EDCS-46300179.summary: This is a funerary inscription listing the ages of several deceased individuals, likely family members, with Sulpicius Ianuarius possibly being the last surviving son.

EDCS-46300183.translation: The temple of Publius Sextus and Rutilia Maxima, calling away from life AT [restored].
EDCS-46300183.summary: This inscription likely records the dedication or restoration of a temple, possibly related to funeral rites or remembrance.

EDCS-46300218.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Julius Felix lived for 31 years. Gaius Julius Saturninus [died] to his son.
EDCS-46300218.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the departed spirits, recording the death of Gaius Julius Felix and mentioning his son, Gaius Julius Saturninus.

EDCS-46300219.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Flavius Protus lived for 82 years.
EDCS-46300219.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the departed spirits, recording the death of Flavius Protus.

EDCS-46300220.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Obesia Urbana lived for 25 years.
EDCS-46300220.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the departed spirits, recording the death of Obesia Urbana.

EDCS-46300248.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Primitiva lived for 45 years. Lies here.
EDCS-46300248.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the departed spirits, recording the death and burial place of Primitiva.

EDCS-46300249.translation: For our Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the divine Septimius Severus Pius Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Augustus, and of Julia Domna Augusta, mother of the camps, senate, and fatherland, grandson of the divine Marcus Antoninus Pius Germanicus Sarmaticus, great-grandson of the divine Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the divine Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the divine Trajan Parthicus and the divine Nerva, Parthicus Maximus, Britannicus Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 18th time, Imperator for the 3rd time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul. The state of Sitifensium Nerviana Antoniniana established [this road] for [the distance of] twelve miles.
EDCS-46300249.summary: This is a dedication inscription by the Roman province of Sitifensium Nerviana Antoniniana, likely marking a road, honoring Emperor Caracalla and his imperial family, dating to around 212-217 CE.

EDCS-46400038.translation: For our Lord Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, two miles.
EDCS-46400038.summary: This is a milestone inscription indicating a distance of two miles for Emperor Gordian III, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-46400041.translation: For our Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, from Tigis [to the] head of the city, two miles.
EDCS-46400041.summary: This is a milestone inscription indicating a distance of two miles for Emperor Maximinus Thrax, likely from the period 235-238 CE.

EDCS-46400167.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. Gaius Atilius Crescens, son of Lucius, of the Arnensis tribe, a veteran and former decurion of an ala, lived 88 years.
